Mr. Ram Narayan Mohanty, Adv.
Mr. Surendra Gautam, Adv.
   UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. This petition is among the oldest cases pending before this
Court. The long history of orders passed in these proceedings
reveals that this Writ Petition initially involved limited
instances of forest degradation but has now encompassed wide-
ranging administrative and ecological issues. The instant order
is being passed to reimagine and streamline the efforts of this
Court towards the goals envisaged in this Writ Petition. The
aim is to ensure that all stakeholders address modern issues

--- Page 30 ---
16
within a contemporary framework, under appropriate judicial
supervision.
A.      THE EVOLUTION OF THIS WRIT PETITION  
2. Shri  T.N.  Godavarman  Thirumulpad  instituted  the  instant
petition in 1995, originally seeking this Court’s intervention
on the rampant depredation of sandalwood trees and other forest
resources in Nilgiris, Tamil Nadu. Though the pleadings were
limited  to  a  particular  forested  region,  this  Court,  in
furtherance of its Constitutional obligation, took cognisance
of systemic lapses and regulatory failures at the national
level, thereby broadening the scope of its efforts in these
proceedings.
3. The first major step in this regard to redefine the term
‘Forest’ was taken through an order dated 12.12.1996. Therein,
this Court recognised that although regulatory mechanisms had
been  developed,  definitional  gaps  had  rendered  them
ineffective. That order also signalled a shift in this Court’s
jurisprudential approach, relying on the opinions of domain
experts rather than bureaucratic views for identifying the
characteristics of a particular Forest Area. The 1996 order was
only the first of many steps taken by this Court in its
endeavour to introduce effective regulatory mechanisms that
truly protect the rich environmental and forest wealth bestowed
upon India.

--- Page 31 ---
17
4. The salient areas in which this Court’s initiative has led to
substantial reform include the Protected Areas, namely, the
National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Conservation Reserves,
and Community Reserves, and Tiger Reserves under the Wildlife
Protection  Act,  1972  and  the  Morphological  Ridge  Forest
existing across the topology of the National Capital Territory
of Delhi. Similarly, robust systems have been developed to
judicially supervise the grant of permissions for diversion of
forest resources, like land and timber, towards essential
development works as well as the management of funds deposited
for the purposes of compensatory afforestation.
5. Many of these systems envisaged and put in place by this Court
have since been legislatively recognised. For instance, the
Central Empowered Committee was originally established by this
Court’s order dated 09.05.2002 as an Expert Body. However, it
has now been formally constituted by the Union of India under
Section  3(3)  of  the  Environment  (Protection)  Act,  1986.
Similarly, this Court had created an ad hoc mechanism to manage
funds received for compensatory afforestation, but now the
Legislature, having recognised the need for proper management
of  such  funds,  has  created  the  National  and  State-level
Compensatory  Afforestation  Fund  Management  and  Planning
Authorities through the Compensatory Afforestation Fund Act,
2016.

--- Page 32 ---
18
6. These efforts have culminated in this Court taking up a wider
supervisory role in the protection of forests in India. The
expansion in scope has evolved the instant proceedings into a
continuing mandamus, leading to the filing of a large number of
Interlocutory Applications (IAs) on a wide range of issues. We
are informed that over 800 IAs are pending for consideration by
this Court.
7. On one hand, such inflation in IAs has prompted this Court to
address new and emerging lacunae in the regulatory schemes and
ensure  that  conservation  initiatives  and  sustainable
development are taken forward in a balanced and uniform manner.
On the other hand, however, this framework, where hundreds of
applications arising from different factual circumstances and
legal claims are filed in the same proceedings, has made the
adjudication  of  such  issues  problematic  and  cumbersome.
Centrifuging all forest related issues of local and executory
nature before this Court has also impacted quick and seamless
access to justice through the decentralised judicial structure
of the High Courts and the Specialised Benches of the National
Green Tribunal. Moreover, the tenor of the original issues
raised in the instant Writ Petition are now at a stark
dissonance  from  the  wide-ranging  and  contemporary  actions
required from this Court at this stage.
8. We acknowledge that the issues before this Court are as wide in

--- Page 33 ---
19
range as the mechanisms for their resolution are varied. It is,
thus, imperative that we give this case a renewed shape and
direction, which would more clearly reflect the supervisory and
directive role played by this Court.
9. To that end, this Court,  vide order dated 16.03.2026, had
requested the learned Amicus Curiae, Mr. K. Parameshwar, Senior
Advocate, to initiate a categorisation exercise in respect of
the IAs pending in this Writ Petition. This was done with a
view  to  initiating  independent  Writ  proceedings  for  the
distinct strata of issues pending consideration before us.
B.      BRIEF NOTE BY THE AMICI  
10. The learned Amicus Curiae has, during the course of hearing,
informed us that the process for categorisation of IAs has been
substantially completed. That being so, they have also supplied
a brief note delineating the background of the petition, the
status of some of the issues pending consideration, and the
proposed recommendations for how the intention of this Court,
recorded in the above-mentioned order, can be crystallised.
11. Before proceeding further, it may be noted that a similar
concern had arisen before us in three long-standing petitions
filed by Shri M.C. Mehta. 1 In those cases, too, a large number
of IAs, regarding different issues but filed in a singular Writ
1  Writ Petition (Civil) No. 13381 of 1984, Writ Petition 
(Civil) No. 4677 of 1985, and Writ Petition (Civil) No. 13029 of 
1985.

--- Page 34 ---
20
Petition, remained pending for this Court’s consideration.
Through judgements dated 11.03.2026 and 12.03.2026, a three-
judge bench of this Court, including both of us, has disposed
of those Writ Petitions by undertaking a systematic procedure
to  identify  the  infructuous  applications  and  creation  of
contemporary suo moto cases to further hear the issues before
this Court.
12. There, approximately 100-450 IAs were pending in each case. The
task  of  identifying  the  relevant  categories  was,  thus,
relatively  straightforward.  Similarly,  it  was  easier  to
determine the most expedient manner of adjudication for each
category of IAs. As such, the complete action of disposal of
the main case and identification, categorisation, and transfer
of the IAs could be completed through a single judgement, with
appropriate directions to all stakeholders.
13. What emerges from the note submitted by the learned Amicus and
the oral submissions made during the course of hearing is that
the instant Writ Petition poses a much larger challenge. We say
so for three reasons.
14. Firstly, over 800 IAs are pending in the instant Writ Petition,
many of them for almost 25 years. This substantially increases

--- Page 35 ---
21
the volume of work required to be undertaken to identify and
categorise each IA.
15. Secondly, various issues raised in the instant Writ Petition
are intricately interlinked. To explicate, some pending IAs
seek permission for initiating a proposed Night Safari, sought
to be operated in Tiger Reserves/Wildlife Sanctuaries that fall
in the Aravalli Region. We may observe that various pending
issues would affect the outcome of such an IA, including (i)
the definition of and the activities permitted in the Aravalli
Region;  (ii) the  boundary  of  the  Tiger  Reserve/Wildlife
Sanctuary,  its  Buffer  Zone/Eco-Sensitive  Zone,  and  the
activities permitted therein; and (iii) the Expert Body Report
on the impact of the specific proposals contained in such IA.
Keeping this in mind, it would not be expedient to proceed with
the disposal of the instant case without fully understanding
the scope of the interconnected issues.
16. Finally, several of the issues raised in these proceedings
would concern the subject matters of Suo Moto Writ Petitions
that have been recently instituted for a similar objective. The
scope of those proceedings would also have to be taken into
account while determining the outcome of the instant Writ
Petition.

--- Page 36 ---
22
17. Keeping this in mind, the learned Amicus has suggested that a
preliminary exercise aimed at collecting necessary information
be  undertaken  before  giving  a  formal  close  to  these
proceedings.  Most  importantly,  it  is  recommended  that  a
questionnaire, prepared by the  Amicus, be circulated to all
Advocates-on-Record (AORs) to obtain such details as may be
required to properly delineate the extent of the questions to
be decided by us. He has suggested that it is only after the
categorisation exercise is completed that this Court should
formally dispose of the instant Writ Petition and initiate new
Suo Moto Writ Petitions on individual issues. We find merit in
this submission of the learned Amicus.
18. Additionally, the learned Amicus has suggested some reforms in
the manner of filing fresh IAs in the proposed suo moto cases.
We shall consider such proposals at a later stage, after
obtaining a holistic view of the expected categorisation of the
pending issues.
C.      DIRECTIONS  
19. In the context of the preceding observations and as a first
step towards infusing efficiency the adjudication process for
the pending issues, we deem it appropriate to issue the
following directions for all the IAs pending in this Writ
Petition:
(i) The learned Amicus shall prepare an online and accessible
questionnaire, seeking details of all IAs pending in the

--- Page 37 ---
23
instant Writ Petition;
(ii) The questionnaire shall elicit, inter alia, the following
details:
(a) The details of the applicants;
(b) The proposed respondents in the IA;
(c) Whether the IA is necessitated by an order of this
Court mandating prior leave of this Court;
(d) The category(s) within which the IA would fall;
(e) Whether the IA has been rendered infructuous due to
the previous orders of this Court or due to lapse of
time;
(f) The reason why the IA cannot be adjudicated by the
jurisdictional  High  Court  or  the  National  Green
Tribunal; and
(g) Any other aspect that the learned Amicus may consider
necessary;
(iii) The online form must also require that a complete set of
the paperbooks of that IA be uploaded along with the
response;
(iv) The  link  to  the  online  questionnaire  form  shall  be
uploaded by the Registrar (Judicial) on the website of
this Court and forwarded for circulation among AORs
through  the  Supreme  Court  Advocates-on-Record
Association. Appropriate steps shall also be taken to
ensure that the same is effectively informed to the
Parties-in-Person;
(v) The concerned AOR or the Party-in-Person, as the case may
be,  who  has  filed  the  IA  shall  fill  the  online
questionnaire on or before 15.06.2026; and
(vi) The learned Amicus shall ensure that all the responses

--- Page 38 ---
24
received from the questionnaire form are fully accessible
to  the  Registrar  (Judicial)  and  other  appropriate
officers in the Registry of this Court for maintenance of
records and further use in categorisation.
20. The above-mentioned form shall also permit responses to be
filed regarding the petitions and appeals that stand tagged
with the instant Writ Petition. These petitions and appeals
shall be dealt with in the same manner as IAs filed in this
petition. We may clarify that if any of the connected cases are
suo moto in nature, the relevant form shall be filled by the
learned Amicus.
21. Once the form has been filled in respect of particular IAs
(including tagged petitions and appeals), the same shall be
listed, after obtaining appropriate directions from the Chief
Justice  of  India  on  the  administrative  side,  before  the
Registrar Court. The Registrar Court shall conduct a hearing on
each IA for determining whether such IA is infructuous; what
category of issues the IA would fall under; and, prima facie,
whether the prayer made therein ought to have been made before
the High Court or the National Green Tribunal, in light of
previous orders of this Court.
22. If the AOR/Party-in-Person does not properly fill such form on
or before the said date, it shall be presumed that the

--- Page 39 ---
25
respective IA has been rendered infructuous and shall be
dismissed as such.
23. The  Registry  may,  meanwhile,  permit  new  Interlocutory
Application(s) to be filed in the instant Writ Petition.
However, in the interest of expediency, such freshly filed IAs
shall  not  be  numbered  or  taken  on  record  till  further
directions of this Court.
24. Ordered accordingly.
25. Once  this  exercise  has  been  completed,  the  instant  Writ
Petition shall be listed before a bench of three judges in the
week commencing 20.07.2026 for further orders, after obtaining
directions  from  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  on  the
administrative side.
(JAYANT KUMAR ARORA)                   (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                   ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
(*) Date in Para 19(v) is changed from 31.05.2026 to 15.06.2026. 

--- Page 40 ---
26
CORRECTED

Mr. Surendra Gautam, Adv.
   UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
26. This petition is among the oldest cases pending before this
Court. The long history of orders passed in these proceedings
reveals that this Writ Petition initially involved limited
instances of forest degradation but has now encompassed wide-
ranging administrative and ecological issues. The instant order
is being passed to reimagine and streamline the efforts of this
Court towards the goals envisaged in this Writ Petition. The
aim is to ensure that all stakeholders address modern issues

--- Page 55 ---
41
within a contemporary framework, under appropriate judicial
supervision.
A.      THE EVOLUTION OF THIS WRIT PETITION  
27. Shri  T.N.  Godavarman  Thirumulpad  instituted  the  instant
petition in 1995, originally seeking this Court’s intervention
on the rampant depredation of sandalwood trees and other forest
resources in Nilgiris, Tamil Nadu. Though the pleadings were
limited  to  a  particular  forested  region,  this  Court,  in
furtherance of its Constitutional obligation, took cognisance
of systemic lapses and regulatory failures at the national
level, thereby broadening the scope of its efforts in these
proceedings.
28. The first major step in this regard to redefine the term
‘Forest’ was taken through an order dated 12.12.1996. Therein,
this Court recognised that although regulatory mechanisms had
been  developed,  definitional  gaps  had  rendered  them
ineffective. That order also signalled a shift in this Court’s
jurisprudential approach, relying on the opinions of domain
experts rather than bureaucratic views for identifying the
characteristics of a particular Forest Area. The 1996 order was
only the first of many steps taken by this Court in its
endeavour to introduce effective regulatory mechanisms that
truly protect the rich environmental and forest wealth bestowed
upon India.

--- Page 56 ---
42
29. The salient areas in which this Court’s initiative has led to
substantial reform include the Protected Areas, namely, the
National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Conservation Reserves,
and Community Reserves, and Tiger Reserves under the Wildlife
Protection  Act,  1972  and  the  Morphological  Ridge  Forest
existing across the topology of the National Capital Territory
of Delhi. Similarly, robust systems have been developed to
judicially supervise the grant of permissions for diversion of
forest resources, like land and timber, towards essential
development works as well as the management of funds deposited
for the purposes of compensatory afforestation.
30. Many of these systems envisaged and put in place by this Court
have since been legislatively recognised. For instance, the
Central Empowered Committee was originally established by this
Court’s order dated 09.05.2002 as an Expert Body. However, it
has now been formally constituted by the Union of India under
Section  3(3)  of  the  Environment  (Protection)  Act,  1986.
Similarly, this Court had created an ad hoc mechanism to manage
funds received for compensatory afforestation, but now the
Legislature, having recognised the need for proper management
of  such  funds,  has  created  the  National  and  State-level
Compensatory  Afforestation  Fund  Management  and  Planning
Authorities through the Compensatory Afforestation Fund Act,
2016.

--- Page 57 ---
43
31. These efforts have culminated in this Court taking up a wider
supervisory role in the protection of forests in India. The
expansion in scope has evolved the instant proceedings into a
continuing mandamus, leading to the filing of a large number of
Interlocutory Applications (IAs) on a wide range of issues. We
are informed that over 800 IAs are pending for consideration by
this Court.
32. On one hand, such inflation in IAs has prompted this Court to
address new and emerging lacunae in the regulatory schemes and
ensure  that  conservation  initiatives  and  sustainable
development are taken forward in a balanced and uniform manner.
On the other hand, however, this framework, where hundreds of
applications arising from different factual circumstances and
legal claims are filed in the same proceedings, has made the
adjudication  of  such  issues  problematic  and  cumbersome.
Centrifuging all forest related issues of local and executory
nature before this Court has also impacted quick and seamless
access to justice through the decentralised judicial structure
of the High Courts and the Specialised Benches of the National
Green Tribunal. Moreover, the tenor of the original issues
raised in the instant Writ Petition are now at a stark
dissonance  from  the  wide-ranging  and  contemporary  actions
required from this Court at this stage.
33. We acknowledge that the issues before this Court are as wide in

--- Page 58 ---
44
range as the mechanisms for their resolution are varied. It is,
thus, imperative that we give this case a renewed shape and
direction, which would more clearly reflect the supervisory and
directive role played by this Court.
34. To that end, this Court,  vide order dated 16.03.2026, had
requested the learned Amicus Curiae, Mr. K. Parameshwar, Senior
Advocate, to initiate a categorisation exercise in respect of
the IAs pending in this Writ Petition. This was done with a
view  to  initiating  independent  Writ  proceedings  for  the
distinct strata of issues pending consideration before us.
B.      BRIEF NOTE BY THE AMICI  
35. The learned Amicus Curiae has, during the course of hearing,
informed us that the process for categorisation of IAs has been
substantially completed. That being so, they have also supplied
a brief note delineating the background of the petition, the
status of some of the issues pending consideration, and the
proposed recommendations for how the intention of this Court,
recorded in the above-mentioned order, can be crystallised.
36. Before proceeding further, it may be noted that a similar
concern had arisen before us in three long-standing petitions
filed by Shri M.C. Mehta. 2 In those cases, too, a large number
of IAs, regarding different issues but filed in a singular Writ
2  Writ Petition (Civil) No. 13381 of 1984, Writ Petition 
(Civil) No. 4677 of 1985, and Writ Petition (Civil) No. 13029 of 
1985.

--- Page 59 ---
45
Petition, remained pending for this Court’s consideration.
Through judgements dated 11.03.2026 and 12.03.2026, a three-
judge bench of this Court, including both of us, has disposed
of those Writ Petitions by undertaking a systematic procedure
to  identify  the  infructuous  applications  and  creation  of
contemporary suo moto cases to further hear the issues before
this Court.
37. There, approximately 100-450 IAs were pending in each case. The
task  of  identifying  the  relevant  categories  was,  thus,
relatively  straightforward.  Similarly,  it  was  easier  to
determine the most expedient manner of adjudication for each
category of IAs. As such, the complete action of disposal of
the main case and identification, categorisation, and transfer
of the IAs could be completed through a single judgement, with
appropriate directions to all stakeholders.
38. What emerges from the note submitted by the learned Amicus and
the oral submissions made during the course of hearing is that
the instant Writ Petition poses a much larger challenge. We say
so for three reasons.
39. Firstly, over 800 IAs are pending in the instant Writ Petition,
many of them for almost 25 years. This substantially increases

--- Page 60 ---
46
the volume of work required to be undertaken to identify and
categorise each IA.
40. Secondly, various issues raised in the instant Writ Petition
are intricately interlinked. To explicate, some pending IAs
seek permission for initiating a proposed Night Safari, sought
to be operated in Tiger Reserves/Wildlife Sanctuaries that fall
in the Aravalli Region. We may observe that various pending
issues would affect the outcome of such an IA, including (i)
the definition of and the activities permitted in the Aravalli
Region;  (ii) the  boundary  of  the  Tiger  Reserve/Wildlife
Sanctuary,  its  Buffer  Zone/Eco-Sensitive  Zone,  and  the
activities permitted therein; and (iii) the Expert Body Report
on the impact of the specific proposals contained in such IA.
Keeping this in mind, it would not be expedient to proceed with
the disposal of the instant case without fully understanding
the scope of the interconnected issues.
41. Finally, several of the issues raised in these proceedings
would concern the subject matters of Suo Moto Writ Petitions
that have been recently instituted for a similar objective. The
scope of those proceedings would also have to be taken into
account while determining the outcome of the instant Writ
Petition.

--- Page 61 ---
47
42. Keeping this in mind, the learned Amicus has suggested that a
preliminary exercise aimed at collecting necessary information
be  undertaken  before  giving  a  formal  close  to  these
proceedings.  Most  importantly,  it  is  recommended  that  a
questionnaire, prepared by the  Amicus, be circulated to all
Advocates-on-Record (AORs) to obtain such details as may be
required to properly delineate the extent of the questions to
be decided by us. He has suggested that it is only after the
categorisation exercise is completed that this Court should
formally dispose of the instant Writ Petition and initiate new
Suo Moto Writ Petitions on individual issues. We find merit in
this submission of the learned Amicus.
43. Additionally, the learned Amicus has suggested some reforms in
the manner of filing fresh IAs in the proposed suo moto cases.
We shall consider such proposals at a later stage, after
obtaining a holistic view of the expected categorisation of the
pending issues.
C.      DIRECTIONS  
44. In the context of the preceding observations and as a first
step towards infusing efficiency the adjudication process for
the pending issues, we deem it appropriate to issue the
following directions for all the IAs pending in this Writ
Petition:
(i) The learned Amicus shall prepare an online and accessible

--- Page 62 ---
48
questionnaire, seeking details of all IAs pending in the
instant Writ Petition;
(ii) The questionnaire shall elicit, inter alia, the following
details:
(a) The details of the applicants;
(b) The proposed respondents in the IA;
(c) Whether the IA is necessitated by an order of this
Court mandating prior leave of this Court;
(d) The category(s) within which the IA would fall;
(e) Whether the IA has been rendered infructuous due to
the previous orders of this Court or due to lapse of
time;
(f) The reason why the IA cannot be adjudicated by the
jurisdictional  High  Court  or  the  National  Green
Tribunal; and
(g) Any other aspect that the learned Amicus may consider
necessary;
(iii) The online form must also require that a complete set of
the paperbooks of that IA be uploaded along with the
response;
(iv) The  link  to  the  online  questionnaire  form  shall  be
uploaded by the Registrar (Judicial) on the website of
this Court and forwarded for circulation among AORs
through  the  Supreme  Court  Advocates-on-Record
Association. Appropriate steps shall also be taken to
ensure that the same is effectively informed to the
Parties-in-Person;

--- Page 63 ---
49
(v) The concerned AOR or the Party-in-Person, as the case may
be,  who  has  filed  the  IA  shall  fill  the  online
questionnaire on or before 31.05.2026; and
(vi) The learned Amicus shall ensure that all the responses
received from the questionnaire form are fully accessible
to  the  Registrar  (Judicial)  and  other  appropriate
officers in the Registry of this Court for maintenance of
records and further use in categorisation.
45. The above-mentioned form shall also permit responses to be
filed regarding the petitions and appeals that stand tagged
with the instant Writ Petition. These petitions and appeals
shall be dealt with in the same manner as IAs filed in this
petition. We may clarify that if any of the connected cases are
suo moto in nature, the relevant form shall be filled by the
learned Amicus.
46. Once the form has been filled in respect of particular IAs
(including tagged petitions and appeals), the same shall be
listed, after obtaining appropriate directions from the Chief
Justice  of  India  on  the  administrative  side,  before  the
Registrar Court. The Registrar Court shall conduct a hearing on
each IA for determining whether such IA is infructuous; what
category of issues the IA would fall under; and, prima facie,

--- Page 64 ---
50
whether the prayer made therein ought to have been made before
the High Court or the National Green Tribunal, in light of
previous orders of this Court.
47. If the AOR/Party-in-Person does not properly fill such form on
or before the said date, it shall be presumed that the
respective IA has been rendered infructuous and shall be
dismissed as such.
48. The  Registry  may,  meanwhile,  permit  new  Interlocutory
Application(s) to be filed in the instant Writ Petition.
However, in the interest of expediency, such freshly filed IAs
shall  not  be  numbered  or  taken  on  record  till  further
directions of this Court.
49. Ordered accordingly.
50. Once  this  exercise  has  been  completed,  the  instant  Writ
Petition shall be listed before a bench of three judges in the
week commencing 20.07.2026 for further orders, after obtaining
directions  from  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  on  the
administrative side.
(JAYANT KUMAR ARORA)                        (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)

2. The petitioner is being prosecuted across

culminated, wherein approximately 5 to 6 cases have 
resulted in acquittal while nearly 35 cases have 
ended in conviction. A large number of trials are still 
pending before various Courts throughout th e 
country. The petitioner has remained in continuous 
judicial custody since his arrest on 27 th June, 2015 
in FIR No. 328/2015 registered at Police Station M.P. 
Nagar, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh and has thus 
undergone incarceration for more than 11 years 
without interruption, as upon grant of bail in one 
matter, he is immediately arrested in another case 
either in the same State or in a different State.

5. It has been submitted that the allegations in all 
the aforesaid FIRs substantially arise out of similar 
transactions, viz., the alleged operation of a Collective 
Investment Scheme ( CIS) through SPPDL and 
associated entities, whereby investors were allegedly 
induced to invest monies in land development 
schemes with promises of high returns and profit 
sharing. 
6. It is alleged that the  petitioner was one of the 
Directors of a company namely , SPPDL, registered 

--- Page 69 ---
5 
 
with the Registrar of Companies at Gwalior, Madhya 
Pradesh, and that the said company indulged in  a 
scheme inviting investments from members of the 
public for purchasing plots/lands at various 
locations with an assurance that such lands would 
be developed and thereafter sold, and that the profits 
generated from the sale of the developed lands would 
be shared with the investors. 
7. For the said purpose, the company, upon 
receiving investments from prospective investors, 
entered into Joint Venture Agreements (JVAs) with 
such investors and also issued bonds and other 
documents purporting to confer equity or proprietary 
interest in the lands allegedly purchased by the 
company. The investors were assured of high returns 
and, in several cases, promises of doubling the 
invested amounts within fixed time periods. 
8. It seems that  the schemes floated by the 
company fell within the ambit of a “Collective 
Investment Scheme” as defined under Section 11AA 
of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act , 
19922, for which prior registration and permission 
 
2 For short, ‘SEBI’. 

--- Page 70 ---
6 
 
from the SEBI was mandatory. Since the company 
had not obtained the requisite registration from 
SEBI, before launching the Collective Investment 
Scheme, the Board initiated proceedings against 
SPPDL and vide interim order dated 26 th December, 
2014 restrained the company and its directors from 
collecting fresh monies from investors or launching 
any new schemes. Subsequently, after investigation, 
SEBI passed a final order dated 29 th July, 2019 
concluding that the activities of SPPDL constituted 
an unregistere d Collective Investment Scheme 3 
conducted in violation of the provisions of the SEBI 
Act and CIS Regulations. 
9. SEBI further found, on the basis of investigation 
and bank account analysis, that the company had 
mobilized at least Rs. 111,54,96,435/ - (Rupees One 
Hundred Eleven Crores Fifty Four Lakhs Ninety Six 
Thousand Four Hundred Thirty Five only) from 
investors, though the company itself had disclosed a 
lesser figure of approximately Rs.84.76 crores. It was 
further observed that the company had failed to 
disclose all its bank accounts and had continued 
 
3 Hereinafter, being referred to as ‘CIS’. 

--- Page 71 ---
7 
 
collection of funds even after the interim restraint 
order passed by SEBI. 
10. According to the prosecution, once the business 
activities of the company were restrained by SEBI 
and the inflow of funds diminished, the company 
defaulted in payment of dividends/profits on the 
amounts invested by investors, which consequently 
led to registration of multiple FIRs in different States 
against the petitioner and other co -accused persons 
and so also the defaulting companies  for offences 
punishable under Sections 420, 406, 409, 467, 468, 
471 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, 
besides offences under the Chit Funds Acts of various 
States, the Prize Chits and Money Circulation 
Schemes (Banning) Act,1978, the Reserve Bank of 
India Act, 1934, the M.P. Nikshepakon Ke Hiton Ka 
Sanrakshan Adhiniyam, 2000, the Chhattisgarh 
Nikshepakon Ke Hiton Ka Sanrakshan Adhiniyam, 
2005 and other allied enactments. 
11. The petitioner, however, has consistently 
asserted that he remained Director of SPPDL only for 
a limited period from 3 rd December, 2009 till 14 th 
June, 2011 and thereafter resigned from the 
company on account of ill health after duly complying 

--- Page 72 ---
8 
 
with the provisions of the Companies Act and filing 
the requisite Form -32 before the Registrar of 
Companies, consequent whereupon his shareholding 
in the company came down to zero. According to the 
petitioner, from the year 2011 onwards, he had no 
role whatsoever in the affairs or functioning of the 
company. 
12. Without prejudice to the above, the petitioner 
through this writ petition under Article 32 of the 
Constitution of India has canvassed an intent to 
amicably settle the disputes with all the 
investors/complainants. The petitioner has claimed 
that the aggregate value of investments made by all 
investors is approximately Rs.110 crores. The 
petitioner, having already undergone incarceration 
for almost 11 years, has prayed that all the FIRs and 
consequential proceedings registered against him in 
different States be clubbed/consolidated and tried by 
one Court in order to avoid multiplicity of proceedings 
and endless incarceration. 
13. Alternatively, learned senior counsel 
representing the petitioner, urged that the petitioner 
is ready and willing to settle with all the investors and 
for this purpose, the petitioner has offered to 

--- Page 73 ---
9 
 
liquidate the lands owned by the petitioner and the 
lands owned/acquired by the company, the details 
whereof are set out in the additional affidavit dated 
25th May, 2026 filed by the petitioner in this Court.

15. In addition, the petitioner also claims to have 
liquid assets  in bank accounts  which are  also 
seized/freezed in connection with various FIRs 
registered against the petitioner. 
16. Learned senior counsel appearing on behalf of 
the petitioner further submitted that this Court may 
explore the possibility of sale of the lands and 

--- Page 78 ---
14 
 
liquidation of assets owned by the petitioner, his 
family members and the various companies operating 
under the Sai Prakash Banner, namely, SPPDL, Sai 
Prakash Financial Advisory Services, Sai Prakash 
Endurance Pvt. Ltd., Bind Finance Ltd., Linc 
Financial Services Pvt. Ltd., Sai Prakash Organic 
Food Ltd., Sai Prakash Power India Ltd., Sai Prakash 
Telecommunications Ltd., Sai Jyoti Mac hinery Pvt. 
Ltd., Sai Prakash Resosys Pvt. Ltd., Sai Prakash 
Developers India Ltd., Rural Industry Development 
Institute, Nain a Telesystems Pvt. Ltd., Lakshraj 
Conveyors Pvt. Ltd. and Chambal Malwa Multi Credit 
Cooperative Society. 
17. It was urged that the cumulative market able 
value of the aforesaid movable and immovable 
properties is in excess of Rs. 250 crores, whereas the 
outstanding liabilities towards the investors are 
stated to be less than Rs. 115 crores. Learned senior 
counsel submitted that if a structured mechanism is 
evolved under the supervision of this Court for 
identification and settlement of claims of genuine 
investors by liquidation of the available assets, the 
entire dispute can be substantially resolved and the  
investors can be indemnified of their losses. 

--- Page 79 ---
15 
 
18. It was canvassed that if the petitioner is granted 
interim bail for the period of three months and a 
Special Committee is formed to sell the lands as well 
as to identify and settle the dues /claims of the 
investors, the larger interest of justice will be served  
and the investors shall be indemnified of their losses 
and the multiplicity of the proceedings across 
jurisdiction would be closed  by way of an amicable 
settlement.  
19. He urged that the petitioner is ready and willing 
to bear the expenses of the Committee. However, for 
this purpose, the petitioner needs to be released from 
custody so that the bank accounts can be mobilized 
and the requisite formalities can be completed.  
20. Having considered the submissions advanced 
by learned senior counsel for the petitioner and 
learned counsel representing the respondents,  and 
taking into consideration the peculiar facts and 
circumstances of the case, particularly the fact that 
the petitioner has remained incarcerated 
continuously for more than 11 years and that the 
alleged disputes pertain substantially to investor 
claims capable of being monetarily quantified and 
settled, we are of the firm opinion that if a genuine 

--- Page 80 ---
16 
 
effort is made to settle the monetary claims of the 
investors, not only will their grievances be requited 
but additionally, a possibility may arise of multiple 
case being closed on account of amicable settlement. 
We, therefore, direct formation of a Specia l 
Committee headed by  Mr. Justice Manindra Mohan 
Shrivastava, Former Chief Justice of Rajasthan High 
Court and Madras High Court . The Special 
Committee shall endeavour to identify and  to settle 
all claims and resolve all the disputes in relation to 
the aff airs of the company Sai Prakash Assurance 
Services Private Limited and the other associate 
companies which indulged in the alleged fraudulent 
activities leading to the registration of the multiple 
FIRs in connection with which the petitioner is in 
custody/stands convicted . For this purpose, the 
petitioner shall proactively cooperate with the Special 
Committee so that the requisite steps including sale 
of assets, etc. can be processed so as to generate the 
funds required for settling all monetary claims of the 
investors/complainants.  
21. The petitioner shall be released on interim bail 
for a period of three months in connection with all the 

--- Page 81 ---
17 
 
FIRs registered against him across  all jurisdictions 
and also in relation to the judgments convicting him  
upon furnishing two surety bonds in the sum of 
Rs.10,00,000/- (Rupees Ten Lakhs only) each and a 
personal bond in the like amount, to the satisfaction 
of the Registrar (Judicial) of this Court. 
22. The Registrar (Judicial) shall ensure that the 
release orders are issued to all Courts/prisons where 
the petitioner ’s custody is required . Upon being 
released from custody, the petitioner shall 
immediately approach the Chairperson of the Special 
Committee and furnish complete particulars relating 
to: 
(i) all FIRs/criminal proceedings pending against 
him and the associated companies; 
(ii) details of the investors and liabilities claimed 
therein; 
(iii) particulars of all movable and immovable 
properties including lands owned by the 
petitioner, his family members and the group 
companies; 
(iv) details of bank accounts, fixed deposits and 
other liquid assets standing in the name of the 

--- Page 82 ---
18 
 
petitioner, his family members and/or the 
associated companies; and 
(v) all relevant title documents, revenue records, 
company records, investor records and any other 
material as may be required by the Special 
Committee. 
(vi) Any additional material/details as may be 
sought for by the Committee for conducting the 
enquiry and settling the claims. 
23. The Registrar (Judicial) shall provide the 
necessary contact details of the Chairperson of the 
Special Committee to the concerned parties. 
24. The Special Committee shall be at liberty to 
convene meetings and proceedings at New Delhi, 
Jaipur, Bhopal, Rewa, Noida and /or such other 
places as may be considered appropriate having 
regard to the pendency of cases, location of investors 
and properties and convenience of stakeholders. 
25. Each State where the FIRs were 
registered/lands are located shall appoint a Nodal 
Officer from the State administration services so as 
to facilitate the proceedings of the Special Committee. 
The Chief Secretaries of the States concerned shall do 
the needful upon receiving a communication from the 

--- Page 83 ---
19 
 
Chairperson of the Special Committee. The petitioner 
shall bear the expenses of the Special Committee for 
the logistics and human resources, etc.; the quantum  
whereof shall be decided by the Chairperson himself.  
The Chairperson shall be provided with all requisite 
secretarial assistance as may be necessary for 
complying with the mandate of this Court.  
26. In addition, thereto, the Chairperson shall be 
entitled to an interim honorarium of Rs. 50,00,000/- 
(Rupees Fifty Lakhs only), half of which shall be paid 
upfront, and half shall be paid at the time of the 
submission of the fi rst report of the Special 
Committee. The Special Committee may take 
assistance of the concerned State Legal Services 
Authorities of the States concerned and their officers 
(Member Secretary and the whole time Secretary) as 
and when felt necessary and the State Legal Services 
Authority (SLSA) shall provide the required 
assistance to the Committee.  
27. The Chairperson shall submit an interim status 
report in a sealed cover on or before 15th September, 
2026 indicating the progress made qua identification 
of investors, verification of liabilities and 
liquidation/valuation of properties. 

--- Page 84 ---
20 
 
28. List this matter  before us  on 21 st September, 
2026 for consideration of the compliance/status 
report to be submitted by the Chairperson of the 
Special Committee. 
29. Interlocutory Application No. 166646  of 2026 
seeking impleadment of the Securities and Exchange 
Board of India (SEBI) as a party respondent in the 
present proceedings is allowed.  
30. Notice be issued to SEBI. The Registry shall 
forthwith communicate a copy of this order to the 
learned Standing Counsel for SEBI as well as to the 
Chairman, SEBI, for information and necessary 
compliance. SEBI shall extend complete cooperation 
and assistan ce to the Chairperson of the Special 
Committee and make available all relevant records, 
documents, and information as may be required for 
the efficient and effective discharge of the 
Committee’s mandate.

NEW DELHI; 
MAY 29, 2026 

--- Page 85 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
 
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION 
 
WRIT PETITION (CRL.) NO(S). 125 OF 2026  
 
 
PUSHPENDRA SINGH  
BAGHEL        ….PETITIONER(S) 
VERSUS 
STATE OF MADHYA  
PRADESH & ORS.    ….RESPONDENT(S) 
 
O R D E R 
Mehta,J. 
1. Heard. 
2. The petitioner is being prosecuted across 
jurisdictions all over India in relation to multiple FIRs 
registered in the States of (i) Rajasthan, (ii) Madhya 
Pradesh, (iii) Uttar Pradesh, (iv) Maharashtra, (v) 
Chhattisgarh, (vi) Gujarat, (vii) Haryana, (viii) Punjab, 
(ix) Uttarakhand and (x) Himachal Pradesh, inter alia 
having substantially identical allegations pertaining 
to the business activities of  a Company named Sai 

--- Page 86 ---
22 
 
Prakash Properties Development Limited 4 and its 
sister concerns. 
3. The petitioner was arraigned as an accused  in 
connection with approximately 149 FIRs and 
criminal cases lodged in different States. Out of the 
said cases, around 40 matters have already 
culminated, wherein approximately 5 to 6 cases have 
resulted in acquittal while nearly 35 cases have 
ended in conviction. A large number of trials are still 
pending before various Courts throughout th e 
country. The petitioner has remained in continuous 
judicial custody since his arrest on 27 th June, 2015 
in FIR No. 328/2015 registered at Police Station M.P. 
Nagar, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh and has thus 
undergone incarceration for more than 11 years 
without interruption, as upon grant of bail in one 
matter, he is immediately arrested in another case 
either in the same State or in a different State. 
4. Learned senior counsel Mr. Vinay K. Garg  
appearing for the petitioner has placed before this 
Court the gravamen of allegation of the principal FIRs 
 
4 Hereinafter, being referred to as ‘SPPDL’. 

--- Page 87 ---
23 
 
and the representative FIRs registered in various 
States, including inter alia:- 
(i) FIR No. 328/2015 registered at Police Station 
M.P. Nagar, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh; 
(ii)FIR No. 496/2018 dated 05.12.2018 
registered at Police Station Lordganj, Jabalpur, 
Madhya Pradesh along with connected FIR No. 
176/2020 dated 24.05.2020 registered at Police 
Station Kotwali, Anuppur, Madhya Pradesh; 
(iii)FIR No. 878/2018 dated 20.11.2018 
registered at Police Station Hindon, District 
Karauli, Rajasthan along with connected FIR No. 
125/2019 dated 06.06.2019 registered at Police 
Station Tibbi, Hanumangarh; FIR No. 90/2019 
dated 12.03.2019 registered at P olice Station 
Bhupalpura, Udaipur; and FIR No. 292/2019 
dated 05.08.2019 registered at Police Station 
Vidhayakpuri, Jaipur City (South); 
(iv) FIR No. 28/2019 dated 13.01.2019 registered 
at Police Station Kotwali, Basti, Uttar Pradesh 
along with connected FIR No. 82/2019 at Police 
Station Hari Parvat, Agra; FIR No. 303/2019 at 
Police Station Aonla, Bareilly; FIR No. 617/2020 
at Police Statio n Nawabad, Jhansi; FIR Nos. 
33/2019, 55/2019 and 57/2019 at Police 
Station Harbans Mohal East, Kanpur City; FIR 
Nos. 480/2020 and 409/2019 at Police Station 
Sungarhi, Pilibhit; FIR No. 119/2019 at Police 
Station Sigra, Varanasi; FIR No. 189/2019 at

at Bareilly/Pandpur; 
(v) FIR No. 24/2018 dated 16.01.2018 registered 
at Police Station Ambala Cantt., Haryana along 
with connected FIR No. 98/2019 at Bhiwani Civil 
Lines; FIR No. 167/2022 at Raipur Rani, 
Panchkula; FIR No. 235/2021 at Hisar City; FIR 

--- Page 88 ---
24 
 
No. 015/2018 at Sirsa City; and Complaint No. 
110/2020 at Narnaul; 
(vi) FIR No. 198/2018 dated 23.10.2018 
registered at Police Station Phase-8, SAS Nagar, 
Punjab;

and FIR No. 86/2024 at Mukhani, Nainital; 
(viii) FIR No. 1 -79/2018 dated 27.09.2018 
registered at Bhavnagar Gangajaliya, Gujarat 
along with connected FIR No. 1 -92/2018, FIR 
No. 1 -293/2018 and FIR No. 1 -285/2018 
registered at Bhavnagar Gangajaliya and R.S. 
Vadaj, Ahmedabad City; 
(ix) FIR No. 13/2019 registered at Police Station 
Sujanpur Tira, Hamirpur, Himachal Pradesh 
along with connected FIR No. 202/2019 
registered at Police Station Jawali-Rangra. 
 
5. It has been submitted that the allegations in all 
the aforesaid FIRs substantially arise out of similar 
transactions, viz., the alleged operation of a Collective 
Investment Scheme ( CIS) through SPPDL and 
associated entities, whereby investors were allegedly 
induced to invest monies in land development 
schemes with promises of high returns and profit 
sharing. 
6. It is alleged that the  petitioner was one of the 
Directors of a company namely , SPPDL, registered 

--- Page 89 ---
25 
 
with the Registrar of Companies at Gwalior, Madhya 
Pradesh, and that the said company indulged in  a 
scheme inviting investments from members of the 
public for purchasing plots/lands at various 
locations with an assurance that such lands would 
be developed and thereafter sold, and that the profits 
generated from the sale of the developed lands would 
be shared with the investors. 
7. For the said purpose, the company, upon 
receiving investments from prospective investors, 
entered into Joint Venture Agreements (JVAs) with 
such investors and also issued bonds and other 
documents purporting to confer equity or proprietary 
interest in the lands allegedly purchased by the 
company. The investors were assured of high returns 
and, in several cases, promises of doubling the 
invested amounts within fixed time periods. 
8. It seems that  the schemes floated by the 
company fell within the ambit of a “Collective 
Investment Scheme” as defined under Section 11AA 
of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act , 
19925, for which prior registration and permission 
 
5 For short, ‘SEBI’. 

--- Page 90 ---
26 
 
from the SEBI was mandatory. Since the company 
had not obtained the requisite registration from 
SEBI, before launching the Collective Investment 
Scheme, the Board initiated proceedings against 
SPPDL and vide interim order dated 26 th December, 
2014 restrained the company and its directors from 
collecting fresh monies from investors or launching 
any new schemes. Subsequently, after investigation, 
SEBI passed a final order dated 29 th July, 2019 
concluding that the activities of SPPDL constituted 
an unregistered Collective Investment Scheme 6 
conducted in violation of the provisions of the SEBI 
Act and CIS Regulations. 
9. SEBI further found, on the basis of investigation 
and bank account analysis, that the company had 
mobilized at least Rs. 111,54,96,435/ - (Rupees One 
Hundred Eleven Crores Fifty Four Lakhs Ninety Six 
Thousand Four Hundred Thirty Five only) from 
investors, though the company itself had disclosed a 
lesser figure of approximately Rs.84.76 crores. It was 
further observed that the company had failed to 
disclose all its bank accounts and had continued 
 
6 Hereinafter, being referred to as ‘CIS’. 

--- Page 91 ---
27 
 
collection of funds even after the interim restraint 
order passed by SEBI. 
10. According to the prosecution, once the business 
activities of the company were restrained by SEBI 
and the inflow of funds diminished, the company 
defaulted in payment of dividends/profits on the 
amounts invested by investors, which consequently 
led to registration of multiple FIRs in different States 
against the petitioner and other co -accused persons 
and so also the defaulting companies  for offences 
punishable under Sections 420, 406, 409, 467, 468, 
471 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, 
besides offences under the Chit Funds Acts of various 
States, the Prize Chits and Money Circulation 
Schemes (Banning) Act,1978, the Reserve Bank of 
India Act, 1934, the M.P. Nikshepakon Ke Hiton Ka 
Sanrakshan Adhiniyam, 2000, the Chhattisgarh 
Nikshepakon Ke Hiton Ka Sanrakshan Adhiniyam, 
2005 and other allied enactments. 
11. The petitioner, however, has consistently 
asserted that he remained Director of SPPDL only for 
a limited period from 3 rd December, 2009 till 14 th 
June, 2011 and thereafter resigned from the 
company on account of ill health after duly complying 

--- Page 92 ---
28 
 
with the provisions of the Companies Act and filing 
the requisite Form -32 before the Registrar of 
Companies, consequent whereupon his shareholding 
in the company came down to zero. According to the 
petitioner, from the year 2011 onwards, he had no 
role whatsoever in the affairs or functioning of the 
company. 
12. Without prejudice to the above, the petitioner 
through this writ petition under Article 32 of the 
Constitution of India has canvassed an intent to 
amicably settle the disputes with all the 
investors/complainants. The petitioner has claimed 
that the aggregate value of investments made by all 
investors is approximately Rs.110 crores. The 
petitioner, having already undergone incarceration 
for almost 11 years, has prayed that all the FIRs and 
consequential proceedings registered against him in 
different States be clubbed/consolidated and tried by 
one Court in order to avoid multiplicity of proceedings 
and endless incarceration. 
13. Alternatively, learned senior counsel 
representing the petitioner, urged that the petitioner 
is ready and willing to settle with all the investors and 
for this purpose, the petitioner has offered to 

--- Page 93 ---
29 
 
liquidate the lands owned by the petitioner and the 
lands owned/acquired by the company, the details 
whereof are set out in the additional affidavit dated 
25th May, 2026 filed by the petitioner in this Court.

15. In addition, the petitioner also claims to have

seized/freezed in connection with various FIRs

16. Learned senior counsel appearing on behalf of 
the petitioner further submitted that this Court may 
explore the possibility of sale of the lands and 
liquidation of assets owned by the petitioner, his 
family members and the various companies operating 
under the Sai Prakash Banner, namely, SPPDL, Sai 
Prakash Financial Advisory Services, Sai Prakash 
Endurance Pvt. Ltd., Bind Finance Ltd., Linc 
Financial Services Pvt. Ltd., Sai Prakash Organic 
Food Ltd., Sai Prakash Power India Ltd., Sai Prakash 
Telecommunications Ltd., Sai Jyo ti Machinery Pvt. 
Ltd., Sai Prakash Resosys Pvt. Ltd., Sai Prakash 
Developers India Ltd., Rural Industry Development 
Institute, Naina Telesystems Pvt. Ltd., Lakshraj 
Conveyors Pvt. Ltd. and Chambal Malwa Multi Credit 
Cooperative Society. 
17. It was urged that the cumulative market able 
value of the aforesaid movable and immovable 
properties is in excess of Rs. 250 crores, whereas the 
outstanding liabilities towards the investors are 
stated to be less than Rs. 115 crores. Learned senior 
counsel submitted that if a structured mechanism is 

--- Page 98 ---
34 
 
evolved under the supervision of this Court for 
identification and settlement of claims of genuine 
investors by liquidation of the available assets, the 
entire dispute can be substantially resolved and the 
investors can be indemnified of their losses. 
18. It was canvassed that if the petitioner is granted 
interim bail for the period of three months and a 
Special Committee is formed to sell the lands as well 
as to identify and settle the dues /claims of the 
investors, the larger interest of justice will be served  
and the investors shall be indemnified of their losses 
and the multiplicity of the proceedings across 
jurisdiction would be closed  by way of an amicable 
settlement.  
19. He urged that the petitioner is ready and willing 
to bear the expenses of the Committee. However, for 
this purpose, the petitioner needs to be released from 
custody so that the bank accounts can be mobilized 
and the requisite formalities can be completed.  
20. Having considered the submissions advanced 
by learned senior counsel for the petitioner and 
learned counsel representing the respondents,  and 
taking into consideration the peculiar facts and 
circumstances of the case, particularly the fact that 

--- Page 99 ---
35 
 
the petitioner has remained incarcerated 
continuously for more than 11 years and that the 
alleged disputes pertain substantially to investor 
claims capable of being monetarily quantified and 
settled, we are of the firm opinion that if a genuine 
effort is made to settle the monetary claims of the 
investors, not only will their grievances be requited 
but additionally, a possibility may arise of multiple 
case being closed on account of amicable settlement. 
We, therefore, direct formation of a Special 
Committee headed by Mr. Justice Manindra Mohan 
Shrivastava, Former Chief Justice of Rajasthan High 
Court and Madras High Court . The Special 
Committee shall endeavour to identify and  to settle 
all claims and resolve all the disputes in relation to 
the affairs of the company Sai Prakash Assurance 
Services Private Limited and the other associate 
companies which indulged in the alleged fraudulent 
activities leading to the registration of  the multiple 
FIRs in connection with which the petitioner is in 
custody/stands conv icted. For this purpose, the 
petitioner shall proactively cooperate with the Special 
Committee so that the requisite steps including sale 
of assets, etc. can be processed so as to generate the 

--- Page 100 ---
36 
 
funds required for settling all monetary claims of the 
investors/complainants.  
21. The petitioner shall be released on interim bail 
for a period of three months in connection with all the 
FIRs registered against him across  all jurisdictions 
and also in relation to the judgments convicting him  
upon furnishing two surety bonds in the sum of 
Rs.10,00,000/- (Rupees Ten Lakhs only) each and a 
personal bond in the like amount, to the satisfaction 
of the Registrar (Judicial) of this Court. 
22. The Registrar (Judicial) shall ensure that the 
release orders are issued to all Courts/prisons where 
the petitioner ’s custody is required . Upon being 
released from custody, the petitioner shall 
immediately approach the Chairperson of the Special 
Committee and furnish complete particulars relating 
to: 
(i) all FIRs/criminal proceedings pending against 
him and the associated companies; 
(ii) details of the investors and liabilities claimed 
therein; 
(iii) particulars of all movable and immovable 
properties including lands owned by the 

--- Page 101 ---
37 
 
petitioner, his family members and the group 
companies; 
(iv) details of bank accounts, fixed deposits and 
other liquid assets standing in the name of the 
petitioner, his family members and/or the 
associated companies; and 
(v) all relevant title documents, revenue records, 
company records, investor records and any other 
material as may be required by the Special 
Committee. 
(vi) Any additional material/details as may be 
sought for by the Committee for conducting the 
enquiry and settling the claims. 
23. The Registrar (Judicial) shall provide the 
necessary contact details of the Chairperson of the 
Special Committee to the concerned parties. 
24. The Special Committee shall be at liberty to 
convene meetings and proceedings at New Delhi, 
Jaipur, Bhopal, Rewa, Noida and /or such other 
places as may be considered appropriate having 
regard to the pendency of cases, location of investors 
and properties and convenience of stakeholders. 
25. Each State where the FIRs were 
registered/lands are located shall appoint a Nodal 

--- Page 102 ---
38 
 
Officer from the State administration services so as 
to facilitate the proceedings of the Special Committee. 
The Chief Secretaries of the States concerned shall do 
the needful upon receiving a communication from the 
Chairperson of the Special Committee. The petitioner 
shall bear the expenses of the Special Committee for 
the logistics and human resources, etc.; the quantum  
whereof shall be decided by the Chairperson himself.  
The Chairperson shall be provided with all requisite 
secretarial assistance as may  be necessary for 
complying with the mandate of this Court.  
26. In addition, thereto, the Chairperson shall be 
entitled to an interim honorarium of Rs. 50,00,000/- 
(Rupees Fifty Lakhs only), half of which shall be paid 
upfront, and half shall be paid at the time of the 
submission of the fi rst report of the Special 
Committee. The Special Committee may take 
assistance of the concerned State Legal Services 
Authorities of the States concerned and their officers 
(Member Secretary and the whole time Secretary) as 
and when felt necessary and the State Legal Services 
Authority (SLSA) shall provide the required 
assistance to the Committee.  

--- Page 103 ---
39 
 
27. The Chairperson shall submit an interim status 
report in a sealed cover on or before 15th September, 
2026 indicating the progress made qua identification 
of investors, verification of liabilities and 
liquidation/valuation of properties. 
28. List this matter  before us  on 21 st September, 
2026 for consideration of the compliance/status 
report to be submitted by the Chairperson of the 
Special Committee. 
29. Interlocutory Application No. 166646  of 2026 
seeking impleadment of the Securities and Exchange 
Board of India (SEBI) as a party respondent in the 
present proceedings is allowed.  
30. Notice be issued to SEBI. The Registry shall 
forthwith communicate a copy of this order to the 
learned Standing Counsel for SEBI as well as to the 
Chairman, SEBI, for information and necessary 
compliance. SEBI shall extend complete cooperation 
and assistan ce to the Chairperson of the Special 
Committee and make available all relevant records, 
documents, and information as may be required for 
the efficient and effective discharge of the 
Committee’s mandate.

Mehta,J. 
1. Heard. 
2. The petitioner is being prosecuted across 
jurisdictions all over India in relation to multiple FIRs 
registered in the States of (i) Rajasthan, (ii) Madhya 
Pradesh, (iii) Uttar Pradesh, (iv) Maharashtra, (v) 
Chhattisgarh, (vi) Gujarat, (vii) Haryana, (viii) Punjab, 
(ix) Uttarakhand and (x) Himachal Pradesh, inter alia 
having substantially identical allegations pertaining 
to the business activities of  a Company named Sai 

--- Page 106 ---
2 
 
Prakash Properties Development Limited 1 and its 
sister concerns. 
3. The petitioner was arraigned as an accused  in 
connection with approximately 149 FIRs and 
criminal cases lodged in different States. Out of the 
said cases, around 40 matters have already 
culminated, wherein approximately 5 to 6 cases have 
resulted in acquittal while nearly 35 cases have 
ended in conviction. A large number of trials are still 
pending before various Courts throughout th e 
country. The petitioner has remained in continuous 
judicial custody since his arrest on 27 th June, 2015 
in FIR No. 328/2015 registered at Police Station M.P. 
Nagar, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh and has thus 
undergone incarceration for more than 11 years 
without interruption, as upon grant of bail in one 
matter, he is immediately arrested in another case 
either in the same State or in a different State. 
4. Learned senior counsel Mr. Vinay K. Garg  
appearing for the petitioner has placed before this 
Court the gravamen of allegation of the principal FIRs 
 
1 Hereinafter, being referred to as ‘SPPDL’.

and the representative FIRs registered in various 
States, including inter alia:- 
FIRs in State of Rajasthan Registered at

5. It has been submitted that the allegations in all 
the aforesaid FIRs substantially arise out of similar 
transactions, viz., the alleged operation of a Collective 
Investment Scheme ( CIS) through SPPDL and 
associated entities, whereby investors were allegedly 
induced to invest monies in land development 
schemes with promises of high returns and profit 
sharing. 
6. It is alleged that the  petitioner was one of the 
Directors of a company namely , SPPDL, registered 

--- Page 109 ---
5 
 
with the Registrar of Companies at Gwalior, Madhya 
Pradesh, and that the said company indulged in  a 
scheme inviting investments from members of the 
public for purchasing plots/lands at various 
locations with an assurance that such lands would 
be developed and thereafter sold, and that the profits 
generated from the sale of the developed lands would 
be shared with the investors. 
7. For the said purpose, the company, upon 
receiving investments from prospective investors, 
entered into Joint Venture Agreements (JVAs) with 
such investors and also issued bonds and other 
documents purporting to confer equity or proprietary 
interest in the lands allegedly purchased by the 
company. The investors were assured of high returns 
and, in several cases, promises of doubling the 
invested amounts within fixed time periods. 
8. It seems that  the schemes floated by the 
company fell within the ambit of a “Collective 
Investment Scheme” as defined under Section 11AA 
of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act , 
19922, for which prior registration and permission 
 
2 For short, ‘SEBI’. 

--- Page 110 ---
6 
 
from the SEBI was mandatory. Since the company 
had not obtained the requisite registration from 
SEBI, before launching the Collective Investment 
Scheme, the Board initiated proceedings against 
SPPDL and vide interim order dated 26 th December, 
2014 restrained the company and its directors from 
collecting fresh monies from investors or launching 
any new schemes. Subsequently, after investigation, 
SEBI passed a final order dated 29 th July, 2019 
concluding that the activities of SPPDL constituted 
an unregistere d Collective Investment Scheme 3 
conducted in violation of the provisions of the SEBI 
Act and CIS Regulations. 
9. SEBI further found, on the basis of investigation 
and bank account analysis, that the company had 
mobilized at least Rs. 111,54,96,435/ - (Rupees One 
Hundred Eleven Crores Fifty Four Lakhs Ninety Six 
Thousand Four Hundred Thirty Five only) from 
investors, though the company itself had disclosed a 
lesser figure of approximately Rs.84.76 crores. It was 
further observed that the company had failed to 
disclose all its bank accounts and had continued 
 
3 Hereinafter, being referred to as ‘CIS’. 

--- Page 111 ---
7 
 
collection of funds even after the interim restraint 
order passed by SEBI. 
10. According to the prosecution, once the business 
activities of the company were restrained by SEBI 
and the inflow of funds diminished, the company 
defaulted in payment of dividends/profits on the 
amounts invested by investors, which consequently 
led to registration of multiple FIRs in different States 
against the petitioner and other co -accused persons 
and so also the defaulting companies  for offences 
punishable under Sections 420, 406, 409, 467, 468, 
471 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, 
besides offences under the Chit Funds Acts of various 
States, the Prize Chits and Money Circulation 
Schemes (Banning) Act,1978, the Reserve Bank of 
India Act, 1934, the M.P. Nikshepakon Ke Hiton Ka 
Sanrakshan Adhiniyam, 2000, the Chhattisgarh 
Nikshepakon Ke Hiton Ka Sanrakshan Adhiniyam, 
2005 and other allied enactments. 
11. The petitioner, however, has consistently 
asserted that he remained Director of SPPDL only for 
a limited period from 3 rd December, 2009 till 14 th 
June, 2011 and thereafter resigned from the 
company on account of ill health after duly complying 

--- Page 112 ---
8 
 
with the provisions of the Companies Act and filing 
the requisite Form -32 before the Registrar of 
Companies, consequent whereupon his shareholding 
in the company came down to zero. According to the 
petitioner, from the year 2011 onwards, he had no 
role whatsoever in the affairs or functioning of the 
company. 
12. Without prejudice to the above, the petitioner 
through this writ petition under Article 32 of the 
Constitution of India has canvassed an intent to 
amicably settle the disputes with all the 
investors/complainants. The petitioner has claimed 
that the aggregate value of investments made by all 
investors is approximately Rs.110 crores. The 
petitioner, having already undergone incarceration 
for almost 11 years, has prayed that all the FIRs and 
consequential proceedings registered against him in 
different States be clubbed/consolidated and tried by 
one Court in order to avoid multiplicity of proceedings 
and endless incarceration. 
13. Alternatively, learned senior counsel 
representing the petitioner, urged that the petitioner 
is ready and willing to settle with all the investors and 
for this purpose, the petitioner has offered to 

--- Page 113 ---
9 
 
liquidate the lands owned by the petitioner and the 
lands owned/acquired by the company, the details 
whereof are set out in the additional affidavit dated 
25th May, 2026 filed by the petitioner in this Court.

15. In addition, the petitioner also claims to have 
liquid assets  in bank accounts  which are  also 
seized/freezed in connection with various FIRs 
registered against the petitioner. 
16. Learned senior counsel appearing on behalf of 
the petitioner further submitted that this Court may 
explore the possibility of sale of the lands and 

--- Page 118 ---
14 
 
liquidation of assets owned by the petitioner, his 
family members and the various companies operating 
under the Sai Prakash Banner, namely, SPPDL, Sai 
Prakash Financial Advisory Services, Sai Prakash 
Endurance Pvt. Ltd., Bind Finance Ltd., Linc 
Financial Services Pvt. Ltd., Sai Prakash Organic 
Food Ltd., Sai Prakash Power India Ltd., Sai Prakash 
Telecommunications Ltd., Sai Jyoti Mac hinery Pvt. 
Ltd., Sai Prakash Resosys Pvt. Ltd., Sai Prakash 
Developers India Ltd., Rural Industry Development 
Institute, Nain a Telesystems Pvt. Ltd., Lakshraj 
Conveyors Pvt. Ltd. and Chambal Malwa Multi Credit 
Cooperative Society. 
17. It was urged that the cumulative market able 
value of the aforesaid movable and immovable 
properties is in excess of Rs. 250 crores, whereas the 
outstanding liabilities towards the investors are 
stated to be less than Rs. 115 crores. Learned senior 
counsel submitted that if a structured mechanism is 
evolved under the supervision of this Court for 
identification and settlement of claims of genuine 
investors by liquidation of the available assets, the 
entire dispute can be substantially resolved and the  
investors can be indemnified of their losses. 

--- Page 119 ---
15 
 
18. It was canvassed that if the petitioner is granted 
interim bail for the period of three months and a 
Special Committee is formed to sell the lands as well 
as to identify and settle the dues /claims of the 
investors, the larger interest of justice will be served  
and the investors shall be indemnified of their losses 
and the multiplicity of the proceedings across 
jurisdiction would be closed  by way of an amicable 
settlement.  
19. He urged that the petitioner is ready and willing 
to bear the expenses of the Committee. However, for 
this purpose, the petitioner needs to be released from 
custody so that the bank accounts can be mobilized 
and the requisite formalities can be completed.  
20. Having considered the submissions advanced 
by learned senior counsel for the petitioner and 
learned counsel representing the respondents,  and 
taking into consideration the peculiar facts and 
circumstances of the case, particularly the fact that 
the petitioner has remained incarcerated 
continuously for more than 11 years and that the 
alleged disputes pertain substantially to investor 
claims capable of being monetarily quantified and 
settled, we are of the firm opinion that if a genuine 

--- Page 120 ---
16 
 
effort is made to settle the monetary claims of the 
investors, not only will their grievances be requited 
but additionally, a possibility may arise of multiple 
case being closed on account of amicable settlement. 
We, therefore, direct formation of a Specia l 
Committee headed by  Mr. Justice Manindra Mohan 
Shrivastava, Former Chief Justice of Rajasthan High 
Court and Madras High Court . The Special 
Committee shall endeavour to identify and  to settle 
all claims and resolve all the disputes in relation to 
the aff airs of the company Sai Prakash Assurance 
Services Private Limited and the other associate 
companies which indulged in the alleged fraudulent 
activities leading to the registration of the multiple 
FIRs in connection with which the petitioner is in 
custody/stands convicted . For this purpose, the 
petitioner shall proactively cooperate with the Special 
Committee so that the requisite steps including sale 
of assets, etc. can be processed so as to generate the 
funds required for settling all monetary claims of the 
investors/complainants.  
21. The petitioner shall be released on interim bail 
for a period of three months in connection with all the 

--- Page 121 ---
17 
 
FIRs registered against him across  all jurisdictions 
and also in relation to the judgments convicting him  
upon furnishing two surety bonds in the sum of 
Rs.10,00,000/- (Rupees Ten Lakhs only) each and a 
personal bond in the like amount, to the satisfaction 
of the Registrar (Judicial) of this Court. 
22. The Registrar (Judicial) shall ensure that the 
release orders are issued to all Courts/prisons where 
the petitioner ’s custody is required . Upon being 
released from custody, the petitioner shall 
immediately approach the Chairperson of the Special 
Committee and furnish complete particulars relating 
to: 
(i) all FIRs/criminal proceedings pending against 
him and the associated companies; 
(ii) details of the investors and liabilities claimed 
therein; 
(iii) particulars of all movable and immovable 
properties including lands owned by the 
petitioner, his family members and the group 
companies; 
(iv) details of bank accounts, fixed deposits and 
other liquid assets standing in the name of the 

--- Page 122 ---
18 
 
petitioner, his family members and/or the 
associated companies; and 
(v) all relevant title documents, revenue records, 
company records, investor records and any other 
material as may be required by the Special 
Committee. 
(vi) Any additional material/details as may be 
sought for by the Committee for conducting the 
enquiry and settling the claims. 
23. The Registrar (Judicial) shall provide the 
necessary contact details of the Chairperson of the 
Special Committee to the concerned parties. 
24. The Special Committee shall be at liberty to 
convene meetings and proceedings at New Delhi, 
Jaipur, Bhopal, Rewa, Noida and /or such other 
places as may be considered appropriate having 
regard to the pendency of cases, location of investors 
and properties and convenience of stakeholders. 
25. Each State where the FIRs were 
registered/lands are located shall appoint a Nodal 
Officer from the State administration services so as 
to facilitate the proceedings of the Special Committee. 
The Chief Secretaries of the States concerned shall do 
the needful upon receiving a communication from the 

--- Page 123 ---
19 
 
Chairperson of the Special Committee. The petitioner 
shall bear the expenses of the Special Committee for 
the logistics and human resources, etc.; the quantum  
whereof shall be decided by the Chairperson himself.  
The Chairperson shall be provided with all requisite 
secretarial assistance as may be necessary for 
complying with the mandate of this Court.  
26. In addition, thereto, the Chairperson shall be 
entitled to an interim honorarium of Rs. 50,00,000/- 
(Rupees Fifty Lakhs only), half of which shall be paid 
upfront, and half shall be paid at the time of the 
submission of the fi rst report of the Special 
Committee. The Special Committee may take 
assistance of the concerned State Legal Services 
Authorities of the States concerned and their officers 
(Member Secretary and the whole time Secretary) as 
and when felt necessary and the State Legal Services 
Authority (SLSA) shall provide the required 
assistance to the Committee.  
27. The Chairperson shall submit an interim status 
report in a sealed cover on or before 15th September, 
2026 indicating the progress made qua identification 
of investors, verification of liabilities and 
liquidation/valuation of properties. 

--- Page 124 ---
20 
 
28. List this matter  before us  on 21 st September, 
2026 for consideration of the compliance/status 
report to be submitted by the Chairperson of the 
Special Committee. 
29. Interlocutory Application No. 166646  of 2026 
seeking impleadment of the Securities and Exchange 
Board of India (SEBI) as a party respondent in the 
present proceedings is allowed.  
30. Notice be issued to SEBI. The Registry shall 
forthwith communicate a copy of this order to the 
learned Standing Counsel for SEBI as well as to the 
Chairman, SEBI, for information and necessary 
compliance. SEBI shall extend complete cooperation 
and assistan ce to the Chairperson of the Special 
Committee and make available all relevant records, 
documents, and information as may be required for 
the efficient and effective discharge of the 
Committee’s mandate.

--- Page 125 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
 
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION 
 
WRIT PETITION (CRL.) NO(S). 125 OF 2026  
 
 
PUSHPENDRA SINGH  
BAGHEL        ….PETITIONER(S) 
VERSUS 
STATE OF MADHYA  
PRADESH & ORS.    ….RESPONDENT(S) 
 
O R D E R 
Mehta,J. 
1. Heard. 
2. The petitioner is being prosecuted across 
jurisdictions all over India in relation to multiple FIRs 
registered in the States of (i) Rajasthan, (ii) Madhya 
Pradesh, (iii) Uttar Pradesh, (iv) Maharashtra, (v) 
Chhattisgarh, (vi) Gujarat, (vii) Haryana, (viii) Punjab, 
(ix) Uttarakhand and (x) Himachal Pradesh, inter alia 
having substantially identical allegations pertaining 
to the business activities of  a Company named Sai 

--- Page 126 ---
22 
 
Prakash Properties Development Limited 4 and its 
sister concerns. 
3. The petitioner was arraigned as an accused  in 
connection with approximately 149 FIRs and 
criminal cases lodged in different States. Out of the 
said cases, around 40 matters have already 
culminated, wherein approximately 5 to 6 cases have 
resulted in acquittal while nearly 35 cases have 
ended in conviction. A large number of trials are still 
pending before various Courts throughout th e 
country. The petitioner has remained in continuous 
judicial custody since his arrest on 27 th June, 2015 
in FIR No. 328/2015 registered at Police Station M.P. 
Nagar, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh and has thus 
undergone incarceration for more than 11 years 
without interruption, as upon grant of bail in one 
matter, he is immediately arrested in another case 
either in the same State or in a different State. 
4. Learned senior counsel Mr. Vinay K. Garg  
appearing for the petitioner has placed before this 
Court the gravamen of allegation of the principal FIRs 
 
4 Hereinafter, being referred to as ‘SPPDL’. 

--- Page 127 ---
23 
 
and the representative FIRs registered in various 
States, including inter alia:- 
(i) FIR No. 328/2015 registered at Police Station 
M.P. Nagar, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh; 
(ii)FIR No. 496/2018 dated 05.12.2018 
registered at Police Station Lordganj, Jabalpur, 
Madhya Pradesh along with connected FIR No. 
176/2020 dated 24.05.2020 registered at Police 
Station Kotwali, Anuppur, Madhya Pradesh; 
(iii)FIR No. 878/2018 dated 20.11.2018 
registered at Police Station Hindon, District 
Karauli, Rajasthan along with connected FIR No. 
125/2019 dated 06.06.2019 registered at Police 
Station Tibbi, Hanumangarh; FIR No. 90/2019 
dated 12.03.2019 registered at P olice Station 
Bhupalpura, Udaipur; and FIR No. 292/2019 
dated 05.08.2019 registered at Police Station 
Vidhayakpuri, Jaipur City (South); 
(iv) FIR No. 28/2019 dated 13.01.2019 registered 
at Police Station Kotwali, Basti, Uttar Pradesh 
along with connected FIR No. 82/2019 at Police 
Station Hari Parvat, Agra; FIR No. 303/2019 at 
Police Station Aonla, Bareilly; FIR No. 617/2020 
at Police Statio n Nawabad, Jhansi; FIR Nos. 
33/2019, 55/2019 and 57/2019 at Police 
Station Harbans Mohal East, Kanpur City; FIR 
Nos. 480/2020 and 409/2019 at Police Station 
Sungarhi, Pilibhit; FIR No. 119/2019 at Police 
Station Sigra, Varanasi; FIR No. 189/2019 at 
Police Station Bhadohi; FIR No. 406/2019 at 
Police Station Cantt., Gorakhpur; FIR No. 
361/2019 and FIR No. 463/2019 at Police 
Station Sigra, Varanasi; and FIR No. 280/2022 
at Bareilly/Pandpur; 
(v) FIR No. 24/2018 dated 16.01.2018 registered 
at Police Station Ambala Cantt., Haryana along 
with connected FIR No. 98/2019 at Bhiwani Civil 
Lines; FIR No. 167/2022 at Raipur Rani, 
Panchkula; FIR No. 235/2021 at Hisar City; FIR 

--- Page 128 ---
24 
 
No. 015/2018 at Sirsa City; and Complaint No. 
110/2020 at Narnaul; 
(vi) FIR No. 198/2018 dated 23.10.2018 
registered at Police Station Phase-8, SAS Nagar, 
Punjab; 
(vii) FIR No. 107/2018 dated 30.10.2018 
registered at Police Station Kotwali, Pithoragarh, 
Uttarakhand along with connected FIR No. 
165/2019 at Police Station Mukhani, Nainital; 
FIR No. 30/2019 at Lohaghat, Champawat; FIR
