IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NOS. 5962 – 5963 OF 2023
SHRI PRASHANT SINGH & ORS              APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH 
AND & ORS.           RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. The present appeals are directed against the common
order  dated  29.04.2020  passed  by  the  High  Court  of
Judicature of Madhya Pradesh at Jabalpur, ( hereinafter,
‘High Court’) in Writ Petition No. 19393/2019 and batch
of WPs and the order dated 29.03.2022 passed in Review
Petition bearing R.P. No. 817/2021 whereby the order
passed in the Writ Petition was affirmed.
2. The grievance of the writ petitioners before the High
Court was with respect to computation of reservation qua
physically  disabled  candidates  (in  short  ‘PWD
candidates’) in the recruitment process for the post of
Assistant  Professor1.  The  challenge  was  laid  to  the
revised advertisement/corrigendum dated 19.08.2019 issued
1  in the Department of Higher Education.
1

--- Page 383 ---
by Madhya Pradesh Public Service Commission - Respondent
No. 2 (hereinafter referred to as “ MPPSC”), whereby the
number  of  vacancies  reserved  for  persons  with
disabilities was reduced from those originally notified
vide advertisement  dated  12.12.2017,  amended  by
corrigenda dated 12.04.2018 and 25.04.2018. The issue
that  arose  for  consideration  was  as  to  whether  the
computation of posts has to be based on the ‘cadre
strength’ or ‘sanctioned or advertised strength’.
3. The  High  Court  vide  impugned  order  held,  the
reduction in the posts reserved for the PWD candidates by
the State in the advertisement is contrary to Sections 332
and  36 3 of  the  Persons  with  Disabilities  (Equal
Opportunities,  Protection  of  Rights  and  Full
Participation)  Act,  1995  (in  short  ‘1995  Act’)  and
Section 344 of the Rights of Persons with Disability Act,
2016 (in short ‘2016 Act’). The High Court directed the
State  to  compute  the  reserved  vacancies  for  PWD
candidates against the total number of vacancies in the
2  Reservation of Posts.
3  Vacancies not filled up to be carried forward.
4  Reservation.
2

--- Page 384 ---
cadre strength. 
4. Further, the State was directed to re-draw the list
of  the  PWD  candidates  after  revising  the  quota  by
computing the posts on the basis of total vacancies in
the cadre strength. Upon filing the review by the State,
it  came  to  be  dismissed  vide  impugned  order  dated
29.03.2022. Hence, these appeals. 
5. This Court issued notice on 29.08.2022 and granted
leave on 12.09.2023, with a direction to maintain status
quo as on the date so far as service of the appellants
are concerned. Further, it was noted that the order would
not create any equity in favour of the appellants. 
6.  The matter was heard at considerable length on
06.03.2024, and posted for further hearing on 13.03.2024.
In  the  meantime,  the  State  was  directed  to  seek
instructions regarding continuation of the appellants who
were working on the strength of the  status quo  order
dated 12.09.2023 passed by this Court. 
7. When the matter was next heard on 27.11.2025, the
learned  counsel  for  State  informed  that  as  per
communication  dated  26.11.2025,  there  are  still  1180
3

--- Page 385 ---
unreserved vacancies out of total 4370 vacancies, copy of
which  was  supplied  to  the  appellants.  They  were
directed  to  seek  instructions  and  file  affidavit
explaining how many persons are already working. 
8. In compliance, the appellants filed affidavit dated
09.01.2026 and stated therein as thus – 
“4. That out of 32 petitioners, three petitioners,
namely Ms. Neha Sahu (Petitioner No. 25), Mr. Rahul
Kumar  (Petitioner  No.  2)  and  Mr.  Vaseem  Ahmed
(Petitioner No. 32) have already left their jobs and
have joined employment elsewhere. It is submitted
that  out  of  remaining  29  petitioners,  ten
petitioners were terminated and subsequently made to
rejoin  from  fresh  date  of  appointment.  These
petitioners are at serial nos. 4, 10, 12, 14, 16,
17, 22, 26, 28 and 31. Petitioner No. 5 and 21 stand
regularized with completed probation. The list-wise
details of their dates of termination are annexed
herewith and are marked as ANNEXURE AA-1 to this
affidavit. These 10 petitioners are working from
2019 onwards till dated. The list-wise details of
their joining are given. 
The remaining 19 petitioners have been continuously
working  from  their  date  of  joining  from  2019
onwards.
5. That, apart from other benefits the probation of
all remaining 27 the petitioners ought to have
completed during the years 2021-2022. However, the
formal order for completion of probation is yet to
be passed by Respondent No. 1. Further, it is also
important to point that other service benefits have
also not been extended to them on the ground that
the  present  litigation  is  pending  before  this
Hon’ble Court. 
6. That, apart from 27 remaining petitioners herein,
there  are  117  similarly  situated  employees  of
4

--- Page 386 ---
Respondent No. 1 who are working on the strength of
the list issued by the Respondent Nos. 1 and 2.
Several such affected persons have approached the
Hon’ble High Court of Jabalpur, where interim stay
orders are operating in their favour……
7. That  the communication dated 26.11.2025 placed
before this Hon’ble Court indicates that there are
still  1180  unreserved  vacancies  out  of  total
vacancies of 4370.”
9. On perusal of the communication dated 26.11.2025 from
the  Office  of  Commissioner,  Directorate  of  Higher
Education, it is revealed that, out of 32 appellants,
only 14 candidates could not be appointed due to non-
availability of the vacancies with respect to the subject
advertisement. The process of appointments by the Higher
Education  Department,  Government  of  Madhya  Pradesh,
Mantralaya, Bhopal, has continued even after 2017. In
this sequence, the MPSC, Indore, advertised 1669 posts of
Assistant Professors in the year 2022 and 1930 posts in
the year 2024. At present, approximately 4370 posts in
different subjects are vacant, out of which around 1180
posts are unreserved. Based on the examination results of
the Assistant Professor Examination, 2017 conducted by
the MPSC, Indore, there are presently no vacant posts
available  for  the  14  petitioners  belonging  to  the
unreserved category. As far as for selecting some of the
5

--- Page 387 ---
candidates and not selecting other, it was reiterated
that  the  posts  available  were  filled  leaving  the
remaining 14 appellants unappointed. 
10. After  hearing  learned  senior  counsel  for  the
respective  parties  at  length,  considering  the  letter
dated 26.11.2025 issued by the Commissioner, Directorate
of Higher Education, and that the State has no objection
to appoint the remaining  appellants being experienced
hands in the available vacancies, if directed. Thus, we
deem it appropriate to exercise our plenary jurisdiction
under Article 142 of the Constitution of India and direct
the State to appoint those appellants who have not been
appointed on the vacant posts of Assistant Professor out
of the posts for which recruitment is going on. In
consequence, we do not find it appropriate to intervene
with the reasoning recorded in the impugned order by the
High Court. 
11. Since the posts concerning the recruitment of year
2017 are already filled, hence, we direct that such
appointment be made on the vacancies of the recruitment
process of the year 2022 or 2024. It is clarified that
6

--- Page 388 ---
the exercise of power in this case under Article 142 of
the Constitution of India in favour of the appellants is
in peculiar facts since they are working for more than
five years, but it shall not be treated as precedent. 
12. With the aforesaid observation and maintaining the
order  of  the  High  Court  these  civil  appeals  stand
disposed of. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand,
disposed of.
…………………………………………………………,J.
     [J.K. MAHESHWARI]
…………………………………………………………,J.
    [ALOK ARADHE]
New Delhi;
May 14, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 408 OF 2025
RIMPI KUMARI …    PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
SURAJ KUMAR SINGH       …    RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R
1. The  present  petition  has  been  filed  by  Petitioner-Wife
seeking transfer of Original Civil Suit bearing No. 562 of 2024 titled
as  “Suraj  Kumar  Singh  v  Rimpi  Kumari”  filed  by  Respondent-
Husband seeking a decree of divorce under Section 13 of the Hindu
Marriage Act, 1955.
2. On  25.07.2025,  with  the  consent  of  the  parties,  the
matter was referred to the Supreme Court Mediation Centre.
3. A mediation report dated 13.11.2025 has been received
from the Supreme Court Mediation Centre alongwith a settlement
agreement dated 12.11.2025.

--- Page 392 ---
4. Efforts made by Ms. Mamta Kumari, learned Mediator
deserves commendation.
5. The parties have settled the dispute in the following
terms:
“6.  Both  the  parties  hereto  have  arrived  at  an
amicable settlement on the following terms and
conditions for dissolution of marriage by mutual
consent:
A. That  the  parties  have  agreed  that  the
Respondent-husband will pay to the Petitioner-
wife a total sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- (Rupees Five
Lakhs Only) towards full and final settlement
of  all  her  claims  towards  her  permanent
alimony, Stridhan, maintenance (past, present,
future) and any other claim whatsoever.
B. Respondent-Husband has agreed to return to
the Petitioner-Wife all household articles and
three  jewellery  articles  which  is  part  of
stridhan and is at present in possession with
Respondent-Husband.  The  Petitioner-Wife
confirms and undertakes that upon receipt of
the Full Amount and articles she will not have
any further claims and all other claimed and
unclaimed amounts on behalf of herself.
I. An  amount  of  Rupees  Five  Lakhs  Only
(Rs.  5,00,000/-)  ("One  Time  Full  and
Final  Payment"),  will  be  paid  by  the
Respondent-Husband  to  the  Petitioner-
Wife under the name of Rimpi Kumari by
way of demand draft/Cash on 05.12.2025
at Supreme Court.

--- Page 393 ---
II. The  Respondent-Husband  shall  deliver
three jewellery articles on the same day
i.e. on or before 05.12.2025.
III. The  Respondent  -  Husband  will  deliver
the household articles to the address of
the  Petitioner  -  Wife  on  or  before
05.12.2025.  The  Petitioner-Wife  or  the
family members of the Petitioner will be
present and confirm the delivery of the
household articles on the receipt, at the
present address of the Petitioner-Wife.
C. Parties agree to delete all photos, videos and
any  other  media  in  any  form  whatsoever
concerning  against  each  other  from  all
electronic devices in their possession and agree
to  not  circulate  any  such  media  by  way  of
message, email, WhatsApp or any other form of
communication in the future.
D. The  Parties  herein  agree  to  take  all  the
necessary steps to ensure that the obligations
under  this  Deed  are  duly  executed  and
completed.  Each  Party  undertakes  to  co-
operate by executing all such documents and
pleadings and affidavits as may be required to
efficaciously close the proceedings between the
Parties.
E. It is further agreed between the Parties that the
expenses incurred between any of the Parties
including  but  not  limited  to  personal  and
travelling  expense  that  may  have  been
incurred, shall be borne by the Parties at their
own cost without burdening the other.
F. It is further agreed that after obtaining decree
of  divorce, both  the Parties hereto shall not
interfere in the life of each other in the future
in any manner whatsoever. The Parties agree
not  to  enter  into  any  further  litigation

--- Page 394 ---
regarding the subject of this Deed against each
other or their family members.
G. It is agreed between the parties that they will
move an application jointly before the Hon'ble
Supreme  Court  under  Article  142  of
Constitution  of  India  for  invoking  inherent
powers of the Hon'ble Supreme Court praying
for divorce by mutual consent as parties are
staying separately since June, 2020 i.e. for the
last  more  than  five  years  and  there  is
irretrievable break down of the marriage.”
6. The following cases, as mentioned in paragraph 5 of the
settlement agreement are pending between the parties:
“5. Whereas the Parties have filed following cases
against each other:
(i) Petitioner/wife has lodged the Warrant or
Summon Case no. 78082 of 2024 titled as
Rimpi Kumari Vs. Suraj Kumar Singh &
Ors. pending before the court of District
Judge,  Gautam  Budha  Nagar,  Uttar
Pradesh.
(ii) Petitioner/wife  has  filed  application  for
maintenance under section 144, Bharatiya
Nagrik  Suraksha  Sanhita,  2023  bearing
Criminal Misc. Case No. 983 of 2024 titled
as Rimpi Kumari Vs. Suraj Kumar Singh
pending  before  the  Ld.  Principal  Judge
family  court  at  Gautam  Budha  Nagar,
Uttar Pradesh.
(iii) Respondent  has  filed  petition  Complaint
case no. 589 of 2025 in the court of Shri

Bokaro (Jharkhand).
(iv) Respondent has filed case bearing no. O.S.
No. 562 of 2024 titled Suraj Kumar Singh

--- Page 395 ---
Vs.  Rimpi  Kumari  before  the  court  of
Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,  Bokaro
Camp  Court,  Bermo  at  Tenughat,
Jharkhand.”
7. As per settlement, the respondent has to pay a sum of
₹5,00,000/-  (Rupees  Five  Lakh)  to  the  petitioner  towards  past,
present and future maintenance and permanent alimony.
8. We  have perused the agreement by  which the parties
have settled their dispute. We have interacted with the petitioner
and the respondent who have attended the hearing through video
conferencing and have been identified by their respective Advocates.
During hearing, the parties have acknowledged the terms of the
settlement. The petitioner states that in terms of the settlement, she
has received all the stated articles and the sum of  ₹5,00,000/-
(Rupees Five Lakh only). Both the parties have requested that in
view of settlement, decree of divorce by mutual consent may be
passed.
9. Having heard learned counsel as also the parties and
considering the terms of settlement, we are of the view that the
settlement deserves to be accepted and accordingly, we dispose of

--- Page 396 ---
this transfer petition and exercise our power under Article 142 of
the Constitution of India with the following directions:
i. Accepting the terms of settlement and as consented
by the parties, we grant decree of divorce by mutual
consent in exercise of our power under Article 142
of the Constitution of India and declare the marriage
solemnized  between  the  petitioner  and  the
respondent on 01.05.2019 as dissolved and they are
at  liberty  to  live  their  lives  with  free  will
independently  without  any  interference  by  other
side.
ii. The  following  cases  pending  between  the  parties
shall stand disposed of without any further action
by them, as settled:
a) Original Suit filed by the Respondent-husband
seeking divorce under Section 13 of the Hindu
Marriage  Act,  1955  bearing  number  Original
Civil Suit No.562 of 2024 titled “Suraj Kumar
Singh  v  Rimpi  Kumari”  pending  before  the

--- Page 397 ---
Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,  Bokaro  Camp

b) Petition filed by Petitioner-wife for maintenance
under Section 144, Bharatiya Nagrik Suraksha

983 of 2024 titled as Rimpi Kumari Vs. Suraj
Kumar Singh pending before the Ld. Principal

    Leave granted. 
2. These appeals arise from a common judgment of the 
High Court of Gujarat dated 26.09.2025, later modified 

--- Page 401 ---
2 
 
on 13.11.2025, whereby compensation awarded by the 
Motor Accident Claims Tribunal, Gondal, in MACP Nos. 
37 and 38 of 2020 was partly enhanced. 
3. The facts are not in dispute. On 12.03.2020, two 
young students, Hirenbhai Bhikhubhai Vekariya and 
Abhaybhai Ravjibhai Parmar, both aged 17 years and 
pursuing their 12th Standard education, met with a 
tragic accident. While returning from their 
examination, the motorcycle ridden by Hirenbhai (with 
Abhaybhai as pillion rider) was struck by a BMW car 
driven in rash and negligent manner. Both sustained 
grievous injuries and succumbed. 
4. FIR was initially registered against an unknown 
vehicle, but investigation revealed the involvement of 
BMW Car No. GJ -03-LB-4554. The Tribunal, by judgment 
dated 18.07.2024, held the driver of the BMW car 90% 
negligent and attributed 10% contributory negli gence 
to the rider of the motorcycle. Compensation of 
₹11,97,600/- (MACP No. 37/2020) and ₹13,30,600/- (MACP 
No. 38/2020) was awarded. 

--- Page 402 ---
3 
 
5. In appeal, the High Court enhanced the compensation 
to ₹13,44,510/ - and ₹14,93,900/ - respectively, but 
while doing so, considered the notional monthly income 
of the deceased at ₹9,000/- only. 
6. Having regard to the fact that both deceased were 
bright students, aspiring to pursue professional 
education in engineering, this Court finds the income 
assessed by the High Court inadequate. Following the 
principles laid down in S. Mohammed Hakkim v. National 
Insurance Co. Ltd.1 and Navjot Singh v. Harpreet Singh2, 
the notional monthly income is fixed at ₹15,000/-. 
7. Regarding the issue of negligence, we have examined 
the findings of the Courts below. We find that the High 
Court’s assessment of the scene of the offence and the 
nature of the head -on collision was based on the 
factual records and evidence presented. Acco rdingly, 
we uphold the High Court about contributory negligence 
as determined in the impugned judgment. 
 
1 (2025 INSC 905)  
2 (2020 SCC OnLine SC 1562) 

--- Page 403 ---
4 
 
8. In the case of Hirenbhai Bhikhubhai Vekariya (MACP 
No. 37/2020), the deceased was survived by three legal 
heirs: his mother, Shardaben Bhikhubhai Vekariya, his 
father, Bhikhubhai Jivrajbhai Vekariya, and his 
brother, Shubham Bhikhubhai Vekariya. Although t he 
Tribunal initially considered only the parents as 
Class-I heirs, we take note of the larger family 
dependency for the assessment of personal expenses. 
9. Consequently, for Hirenbhai, the addition of 40% 
towards future prospects and the multiplier of 18 are 
maintained, and a deduction of 1/3 towards personal 
expenses is applied. The revised calculation is as 
follows:

11. Accordingly, the appeals are allowed.  
12.  In view of the above, we hereby award a total 
compensation of Rs. 31,57,100/- (Rupees Thirty-One Lakh 
Fifty-Seven Thousand One Hundred Only) to the legal 
heirs of Hirenbhai Bhikhubhai Vekariya (MACP No. 
37/2020 / FA No. 363/2025) and a total compensation 
amount of   Rs. 31,57,100/- (Rupees Thirty -One Lakh 
Fifty-Seven Thousand One Hundred Only)  to the legal 
heirs of Abhaybhai Ravjibhai Parmar (MACP No. 38/2020 
/ FA No. 370/2025), respectively.  The impugned award 
stands modified accordingly. 
13. The respondent –Insurance Company (Respondent      
No. 3) shall deposit the enhanced compensation, after 
adjusting sums already paid within eight weeks. The 
amount shall carry interest @ 9% per annum from the 
date of filing of claim petitions till realization. 
14. The amount be directly remitted into the bank 
account of the claimants. The particulars of the bank 
account are to be immediately supplied by the learned 
counsel for the appellants to the learned counsel for 
the respondent. The amount be remitted positively 

--- Page 407 ---
8 
 
within a period of eight weeks thereafter. The period 
of delay in filing this appeal shall be excluded during 
the computation of interest.  
15. We further direct that, if the claimant has not 
yet received the amount in terms of the judgment passed 
by the High Court, the same shall be remitted within 
eight weeks. However, if the amount already stands 
deposited, either before the High Court or the 
Tribunal, the same shall be disbursed to the claimants 
together with the entire interest accrued thereon. 
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand 
disposed of.

--- Page 416 ---
1
              REPORTABLE
  
  IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal No(s).  8212-8213   OF 2026   
(Arising out of SLP (C) No.34898-34899 of 2014)
  GOPALAKRISHNA SURAPANENI                         APPELLANT(s)
                               VERSUS
  ANURADHA SURPANENI MAIDEN                        RESPONDENT(s)
O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellant is also present in person. The respondent has
joined the proceedings through virtual mode. 
4. The  appellant  has  moved  the  Court  against  the  order
impugned by which the High Court has upheld the dismissal of
the petition filed by the appellant before the Family Court
seeking divorce from the respondent-wife. 
5. Learned Senior counsel for the appellant submitted that the

--- Page 417 ---
2
parties are living separately since the year 2003 and despite
his efforts, they could not be reunited. It was submitted that
the conduct of the respondent-wife right from the beginning has
been one of harassment towards the appellant for the reason
that when both of them were living abroad, she started making
wild and scandalous allegations against him due to which,
ultimately, the appellant lost his job and had to relocate to
India to live with his parents at Hyderabad. It was submitted
that due to the constant harassment faced, the family had moved
to  Andhra  Pradesh,  after  selling  the  Hyderabad  residence.
Thereafter,  the father of the appellant has died and he is
living with his mother at the house of his sister after coming
back to Hyderabad, as he has no permanent residence/house of
his own, or in the name of his parents, as the house of the
parents of the appellant was sold in Hyderabad and they shifted
to Andhra Pradesh and the money which they got from the sale
was used for the treatment of the father of the appellant who
later on passed away. 
6. It was contended that in such background, the appellant has
now realized that he has no future with the respondent-wife and
has gone on the path of self-realization and does not want his
peace  to  be  disturbed  at  this  juncture  in  life.  It  was
submitted that after the separation, he has given more than Rs.

--- Page 418 ---
3
40,00,000/- (Rupees Forty Lakhs) to the respondent-wife for her
and their daughter's upkeep. Further, it was contended that in
terms of the earlier order of this Court, Rs. 68,00,000/-
(Rupees Sixty Eight Lakhs) has been deposited in the Registry
of the Court, which now has increased due to accumulation of
interest to approximately Rs. 75,00,000/- (Rupees Seventy Five
Lakhs). On a query of the Court as to what would be the quantum
he would be ready to further give to the respondent-wife by way
of a one-time permanent settlement, upon instructions, learned
counsel for the appellant submitted that initially the amount
indicated to him was Rs. 40,00,000/- (Rupees Forty Lakhs), but
now the appellant has agreed to a sum of Rs. 60,00,000/-
(Rupees Sixty Lakhs). 
7. On a query as to whether the appellant contends that he
should be made free of any responsibility towards the daughter
who is not before us, he fairly submits that the rights of the
daughter qua the father, including that on his estate/ancestral
properties would not be diminished and further that he would
also pitch in with his contribution at the time of the marriage
of his daughter. 
8. The respondent appearing in person vehemently opposed the
prayer seeking divorce. It was contended that the issues raised
by the appellant are frivolous and in fact fictitious as no

--- Page 419 ---
4
such incident occurred. It was further contended that the
respondent is totally opposed to grant of divorce, more so
because of societal pressure. On a query of the Court as to
what were her real differences, when clearly relationships
cannot be restored between the parties by passing of orders and
the Court was of the tentative opinion that the marriage has
irretrievably broken down, the respondent-wife could not come
up with any satisfactory explanation. 
9.  With regard to the quantum of the money being offered by
the appellant to the respondent, the respondent was not ready
to accept the fact that this would be by way of a permanent
settlement in terms of a divorce granted to the parties. 
10. The Court thus, was left in a peculiar situation where it
had to take a call as to whether interference was required or
not. The Court may indicate here that in such matters and at a
juncture where the stance of the parties are absolutely hard
and totally inflexible, the Court has to take a hard decision
which may not be acceptable to both the parties. However, the
Court has to look at the matter objectively, more so, for the
reason that both the parties have a life ahead which it is for
them  to  choose  how  they  want  to  spend  and  forcing  a
relationship, that too of husband and wife, cannot be done by
judicial proceedings. The Court thus has taken a very realistic

--- Page 420 ---
5
view in the matter and going by what has been stated above, has
come to the conclusion that the marriage between the parties
being dead for all practical purposes has to be nullified. 
11. Accordingly,  exercising  our  power/jurisdiction  under
Article 142 of the Constitution of India, we grant a decree of
divorce to the parties on the ground of irretrievable breakdown
of marriage. Registry to prepare the decree accordingly. 
12. However, the said grant of divorce is subject to the
appellant  paying/transferring  an  amount  of  Rs.  60,00,000/-
(Rupees Sixty Lakhs)  in favour of the respondent-wife within
two months from today, as has been undertaken before the Court
and proof thereof be filed before this Court. The amount lying
with the Registry of Rs. 68,00,000/- (Rupees Sixty Eight Lakhs)
along  with  whatever  interest  may  have  accrued  in  the
interregnum be paid to the respondent by the Registry upon
completion of the required formalities within two weeks from
today. 
13. We also record that their daughter, who is not before us,
shall not be denuded of her rights which are crystallized due
to her being the biological daughter of the appellant. Further,
we also record that the appellant has agreed that he would also
contribute his share in the marriage of the daughter whenever
the same takes place.

--- Page 421 ---
6
14. As we have been informed that there is one case filed by
the appellant which is pending at Hyderabad being FCA No.93 of
2019, the same stands quashed.
15. The appeals stand allowed in the aforementioned terms.  
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
          
                                        .......................J.
(AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH)
                          .......................J.
(R. MAHADEVAN)
NEW DELHI
May 27, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  2964/2026
@  SLP(Crl) No. 010726 / 2026     
@  Diary No. 32003/2026  
MUTHUKUMAR S                                     Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF TAMIL NADU & ANR.                       Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Permission to file appeal is granted.
2. Leave granted.
3. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
4. This  appeal  impugns  an  order  of  the
Madras  High  Court,  Madurai  Bench  dated
07.05.2026  in  Criminal  Appeal  (MD)  No.
559/2026, whereby the High Court has set aside
the order of the Sessions Judge (Special Court)
dated 30.04.2026 by which the bail granted to
the second respondent in FIR No. 302/2024 was
cancelled.
5. The case of the appellant, who is the
informant, is that the second respondent was
granted  bail  vide order  dated  14.10.2024
subject to certain conditions. One of those
1

--- Page 433 ---
conditions was that the bail applicant shall
stay at Permabalur District and shall appear
and sign before the Police Station concerned
daily at 9:00 am and 5:00 pm until further
orders. Another condition was that the bail
applicant shall not instigate any other person
of his village or relatives or any of his
associates to trouble the de facto complainant
and shall not tamper the witnesses besides he
shall co-operate in the investigation. It was
provided in the bail granting order that on
default in fulfilling any of the conditions,
the bail would be liable to be cancelled.
6. According to the appellant, the State had
moved an application for cancellation of the
bail on violation of the bail conditions. One
of the conditions allegedly violated was of not
marking  presence  at  the  Police  Station
concerned. Besides above, it was alleged that
the  bail  applicant  had  threatened  the
witnesses.
7. The  learned  Additional  Sessions  Judge
cancelled the bail vide order dated 09.09.2025.
While  passing  the  cancellation  order,  the
2

--- Page 434 ---
learned Additional Sessions Judge took notice
of the grounds taken for cancellation, such as
the  bail  applicant  had  absconded  and  was
tampering with the witnesses. However, while
canceling  the  bail,  the  learned  Additional
Sessions Judge recorded its satisfaction only
regarding non-fulfillment of the condition of
marking  presence  at  the  Police  Station
concerned.
8. Aggrieved by the order canceling the bail
bonds, the second respondent (i.e., the bail
applicant) filed an appeal under Section 14A of
the  Scheduled  Castes  and  Scheduled  Tribes
(Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989. The said
appeal was allowed by the impugned order, and
the second respondent who had in the meantime
been taken into custody was ordered to be
released  on  executing  a  bail  bond  of  Rs.
7,000/-. 
9. The learned counsel for the appellant has
assailed the order passed by the High Court,
inter alia, on the ground that the High Court
failed  to  consider  whether  the  second
respondent had violated another condition of
3

--- Page 435 ---
bail, namely, of not tampering the witnesses,
particularly when there were allegations that
the bail applicant (i.e., second respondent)
had tampered with the witnesses. Besides above,
it is argued that it is a case of brutal murder
but neither the trial court nor the High Court
has considered the relevant material existing
on record against the second respondent before
admitting him to bail.
10. Per contra, the learned counsel for the
second respondent submitted that the original
bail granting order was not challenged; it is
the alleged violation of the bail conditions
which  had  formed  the  basis  for  seeking
cancellation and, therefore, the merits of the
bail granting order need not be addressed at
this stage.
11. Be that as it may, in our view, when the
allegations were that the second respondent had
been tampering with the witnesses, the High
Court before setting aside the order of the
learned Additional Sessions Judge ought to have
considered that aspect even though it was not
specifically taken as a ground to cancel bail
4

--- Page 436 ---
as the learned Additional Sessions Judge deemed
it appropriate to cancel the bail bonds on non-
fulfillment of one of the conditions.
12. For  the  above  reason,  we  deem  it
appropriate to set aside the impugned order
passed by the High Court and restore Criminal
Appeal No. 559/2026 of the second respondent to
its original number for a fresh consideration
in accordance with law after giving opportunity
of hearing to all parties concerned. While
deciding  the  appeal  the  High  Court  shall
consider whether there exists any material to
form an opinion that the second respondent has
tampered  with  the  witnesses  and  thereby
violated one of the conditions of the bail.
13.  The  criminal  appeal  of  the  second
respondent  shall  be  decided  expeditiously
within a period of six weeks from the date a
copy of this order is placed before the High
Court.
14. In the meantime, the second respondent
shall surrender by 10.06.2026.
15. It  is  made  clear  that  we  have  not
expressed any opinion as to whether the second
5

--- Page 437 ---
respondent has violated the bail conditions or
not.
16.    With  the  aforesaid  observations/
directions, the appeal stands disposed of.
17. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall
also stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………………….J
[MANOJ MISRA]
…………………………………………………………….J
[MANMOHAN]
New Delhi;
May 29, 2026.

--- Page 440 ---
1
    IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 2831 OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.3923 of 2026)
SHAMBHU MISHRA                                     APPELLANT(s)
                               VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.   
R1  :State  Of  Bihar,  Through  SHO  Pandaul  Police  Station,
Madhubani Sadar, Madhubani, Bihar
R2 : Pradeep Kumar Thakur @ Pradeep Thakur
 RESPONDENT(s)
O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. Leave granted.
3. The present petition seeks cancellation of anticipatory
bail granted to the respondent No.2 in Cr. Misc. No. 26709 of
2025 passed by the High Court vide order dated 11.07.2025 in
connection with Pandaul P.S. Case No. 137 of 2023 registered
under Sections 341, 323, 324, 307, 354. 379, 504, 34 of the

--- Page 441 ---
2
Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC).
4. The allegation against the respondent No.2 is that he,
along with others, had attacked the informant/appellant with
sticks on the head resulting in serious injuries.
5. Learned  counsel  for  the  appellant  submitted  that  the
allegation being serious in nature which was corroborated by
the medical report, the grant of anticipatory bail to the
respondent No.2 was not justified. It was submitted that the
respondent No.2 has six other criminal antecedents under grave
Sections of the IPC.
6. Learned counsel also submitted that the Court may also take
note of the fact that the initial medical report of the
appellant opined that the injury was simple in nature, despite
recording the fact that there were some changes in the brain
and later a duly constituted Medical Board has also found such
changes and has ultimately opined that the injury was grievous
in nature. It was submitted that the allegation against the
respondent No.2 being that of assault by a lathi which is a
hard and blunt substance is corroborated in both the medical
reports. 
7. Learned counsel for the State supported the contention of

--- Page 442 ---
3
learned counsel for the appellant and prayed that the Court may
interfere in the order granting anticipatory bail. 
8. Learned counsel for the respondent No.2 submitted that the
order initially granting anticipatory bail to the respondent
No.2 was challenged by the appellant in a proceeding before
this Court in Special Leave Petition(Criminal) Diary No.6814 of
2025 which was dismissed as withdrawn by order dated 07.03.2025
with liberty to the appellant to move the High Court for
cancellation of the bail granted to the respondent No.2 by the
High Court. Thus, it was contended that once upon such petition
been filed before the High Court and the same being rejected,
the appellant could not have moved again before this Court as
no leave was either sought for or granted by this Court
earlier. It was further contended that the Court may not be
persuaded by the antecedents as every person is entitled to a
future and the respondent No.2 shall abide by any condition
which the Court may deem appropriate and impose on him. 
9. Having considered the matter in its entirety from various
angles and going through the material on record, we find that
the order granting anticipatory bail to the respondent No.2
cannot be sustained. 
10. Firstly, dealing with the issue of maintainability, the

--- Page 443 ---
4
Court finds the submission to be erroneous. This Court had
given liberty to the appellant to move for cancellation of bail
and not to seek review of the order granting bail. Thus, it was
a fresh cause of action and a fresh order was passed by the
High Court, which obviously did not require liberty for filing
appeal  before  this  Court  in  the  earlier  proceeding.
Accordingly, the objection stands rejected. 
11. Coming to the merits of the matter, in the background of
the allegations made and corroborated by two medical reports,
with  the  fact  that  the  respondent  No.2  has  six  criminal
antecedents under various grave Sections of the IPC, it has
rightly been contended by learned counsel for the appellant
that anticipatory bail ought not to have been granted in the
present case. 
12. Accordingly, for the reason aforesaid, the appeal stands
allowed.  The  impugned  order  dated  11.07.2025  granting
anticipatory bail to the respondent No.2 stands set aside. The
respondent No.2 is directed to surrender before the Court
below, latest within two weeks from today failing which Trial
Court to take all coercive steps in accordance with all for
production of the respondent. 
13. It goes without saying that any observation made in the

--- Page 444 ---
5
present order shall not prejudice the case of the respondent
No.2 during trial. 
14. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
          
                                        .......................J.
(AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH)
                          .......................J.
(R. MAHADEVAN)
NEW DELHI
MAY 25, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 4580 of 2026)
RAJ PRATAP @ SONU … APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. … RESPONDENT
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 6625 of 2026)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. After  hearing  learned  counsel  for  the
parties, we find that the appellants have already
served more than half of the sentence as awarded
for the purpose of charge under Section 304 first
part read with Sections 34, 504 and 506(2) of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 and Section 25 of the Arms
Act.  Considering the aforesaid, subject to final
outcome of the criminal appeal, without expressing
any opinion on the merits of the case,  we deem it
appropriate  to  suspend  the  substantive  jail
sentence of the appellants.
1SLP (Crl) No.4580/2026

--- Page 462 ---
3. Accordingly, we direct that substantive jail
sentence of the appellants shall remain suspended
and the appellants be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as may be deemed fit and
imposed by the trial court.
4. We clarify and direct that after the release,
in case the appellants are found indulged in any
criminal case, the State Government would be at
liberty to take recourse as permissible seeking
cancellation of suspension of sentence.
5. Accordingly, the appeals stand disposed of.
Pending applications, if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
…………………………………………………,J.
[J.K. MAHESHWARI]
………………………………………………………,J.
[ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (C) NO. 1085/2025
SURABHI SAVYASAACHI MUKE                PETITIONER
VERSUS
SAVYASAACHI VIJAYKUMAR MUKE          RESPONDENT
O R D E R
This Transfer Petition is filed by the petitioner/wife,
seeking the following reliefs:
“A)  Transfer  of  the  Petition  filed  by  the
Respondent-husband under Section 13(1)(ia) of the
Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 being titled as Marriage
Petition  No.  2036  of  2024  titled  as  “Mr.
Savyasaachi Vijaykumar Muke Versus Mrs. Surabhi
Savyasaachi Muke” pending before 4 th Jt Civil Judge
(S.D.)  Kalyan,  Kalyan,  Thane  to  the  Principal
Family  Judge,  Family  Court,  Gwalior,  Madhya
Pradesh. 
B) pass such  order and further order as this
Hon’ble Court may deem fit and necessary in the
facts and circumstances of the present case.”
2. During the pendency of this transfer petition, the matter
was referred to the Supreme Court Mediation Centre in order to
explore the possibility of a settlement between the parties. 
3. Learned counsel for the respective parties submitted that
1

--- Page 469 ---
the parties have settled all their disputes and decided to
part ways and seek dissolution of their marriage by a decree
of divorce by mutual consent subject to certain terms and
conditions which are delineated in the Settlement Agreement
dated 22.01.2026 arrived at between the parties before the
Supreme Court Mediation Centre. 
4. Learned counsel for the parties submitted that a sum of
Rs.15,00,000/- (Rupees fifteen lakhs only) has been paid by
the respondent/husband to the petitioner/wife in full and
final settlement of all her claims on the respondent. The
petitioner has acknowledged receipt of Rs.15,00,000/- and all
the items mentioned in Annexures A and B to the Settlement
Agreement dated 22.01.2026
5. Learned counsel for the respective parties also submitted
that there are as many as four cases  pending between the
parties. In view of the amicable settlement arrived at between
the parties, the following cases may be quashed:
Sr.

6. Learned counsel for the respective parties also submitted
that a joint application under Article 142 of the Constitution
of India filed by the parties seeking dissolution of their
marriage by a decree of divorce by mutual consent as well as
the Settlement Agreement dated 22.01.2026 m ay be taken on
record  and  relief  may  be  granted  to  the  parties  and
consequently, the Transfer Petition may be disposed of.
7. The parties are present before this Court through video
conferencing facility and they have been identified by their
respective counsel.
3

--- Page 471 ---
8. When we queried the parties, they stated that they have
indeed arrived at a settlement of their disputes as per the
Settlement Agreement dated 22.01.2026 arrived at before the
Supreme Court Mediation Centre and would abide by the terms of
the said settlement agreement. They also stated that they have
decided to part ways and seek dissolution of their marriage by
a decree of divorce by mutual consent. Therefore, they have
filed  the  joint  application  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution of India which may be allowed.
9. In the above backdrop, we have taken on record Settlement
Agreement dated 22.01.2026 formulated before the Supreme Court
Mediation Centre as well as the joint application filed by the
parties under Article 142 of the Constitution of India.
10. We have noted the terms and conditions of the Settlement
Agreement as well as the prayers sought for by the parties. On
perusal  of  the  terms  and  conditions  of  the  settlement
agreement, we find that they are lawful and there is no legal
impediment in accepting the same.  We also note that a sum of
Rs.15,00,000/- (Rupees fifteen lakhs only) has been paid by
the respondent/husband to the petitioner/wife in full and
final settlement of all her claims against the respondent
herein. 
11. In view of the fact that the parties intend to part ways
4

--- Page 472 ---
and seek dissolution of their marriage by a decree of divorce
by mutual consent, the cases referred to above shall stand
quashed.  Consequently,  the  marriage  between  the  parties
solemnised on 06.06.2022 at Bhopal stands dissolved by a
decree of divorce by mutual consent.  
12. The terms of settlement agreement dated 22.01.2026 shall
be read as a part and parcel of this order. 
Registry to draw up a decree in the aforesaid terms.
Consequently, the Transfer Petition is disposed of in the
aforesaid terms.

  [B.V. NAGARATHNA]
………………………………………………J.
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
NEW DELHI
MAY 25, 2026

IA No. 148934/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED

Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) DY.No.14261/2026(Item 69)
IA No. 154100/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE

Date : 22-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Ravi Prakash Mehrotra, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Shashi Ranjan, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhishek Vikas, AOR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
SLP (Crl.)  No(s).  9034/2026
1. Through this petition, the petitioner seeks to impugn the
order of the High Court dated 30.04.2026 by which the stay
application of the petitioner has been rejected.
1

--- Page 495 ---
2. The case of the petitioner is that he has suffered two ex-
parte orders in the proceedings instituted by the second respondent
under Section 12 of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence
Act, 2005.  It has been submitted that by an ex-parte order dated
14.12.2020, the learned Magistrate has passed protective orders in
respect of two flats when at the most it ought to have been in
respect of one flat.  It is the case of the petitioner that taking
advantage of the protective order, the second respondent has let
out a flat and, therefore, the High Court ought to have modified
the order passed by the court below.
3. As we notice that the proceedings are pending before the first
Court, the appropriate course for the petitioner is to seek for
modification  of  the  interim  orders  passed  therein  by  moving
appropriate  application.   It  is  expected  that  if  any  such
application  is  submitted  by  the  petitioner,  the  Court  shall
entertain such application and after hearing the parties pass
appropriate orders.  We also clarify that while dealing with any
such application for modification of the ex-parte order, the court
shall not be prejudice by any observations made by the Appellate
Court as well as the High Court in the orders impugned.
4. With the aforesaid observations, the special leave petition
stands disposed of.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
2

--- Page 496 ---
Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) DY.No.14261/2026
1. The learned counsel for the petitioner prays to withdraw the
special leave petition.
2. The special leave petition is dismissed as withdrawn.
  (KAVITA PAHUJA)                               (SAPNA BANSAL)

--- Page 506 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
   CIVIL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
   (Arising out of SLP(C) No.13947 of 2026)
    
UNION OF INDIA & ANR.                            APPELLANTS
A1 : UNION OF INDIA
A2 : UNION PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION
                                VERSUS
SHUBHAM AGARWAL                                      RESPONDENT
O R D E R
Heard Mr. S.D. Sanjay, learned Additional Solicitor General
(ASG) for the appellants and Mr. M.R. Shamshad, learned senior
counsel for the respondent. 
2. Leave granted.
3. The only issue which is required to be considered by this
Court is whether the respondent in reality is suffering from
permanent hearing impairment of 40% and above or not. Numerous
Boards have been constituted, including Appellate Board, but the
reports/results vary to such an extent that no final opinion is
possible based on them.
4. Finally, with the consent of the parties,  vide  order dated
24.04.2026 of this Court, the matter was referred to the Rajiv
Gandhi Government General Hospital, Chennai, where the Dean of the

--- Page 507 ---
2
said  Hospital  was  directed  to  constitute  a  Board  of  ENT
Specialist/Audiologists having expertise in the field of hearing
disability  for  assessing  the  hearing  of  the  respondent  and
determine  whether  he  suffers  from  any  disability  of  hearing
impairment and if so, to what extent.
5. In  terms  of  the  aforesaid  order,  a  Medical  Board  was
constituted,  and  the  respondent  also  appeared  before  it  on
20.05.2026 at Chennai.
6. A copy of the report has been submitted, in sealed cover, to
the Court by Mr. S.D. Sanjay, learned ASG. The said report is dated
22.05.2026, i.e., today. As the said report has been issued today
itself, the same was transmitted through digital mode to Mr. S.D.
Sanjay, learned ASG and a hard copy of the same has been placed
before us by the learned ASG.
7. Perusal of the same indicates that initially the respondent
appeared in the said Hospital and a preliminary examination was
also  conducted.  Thereafter,  he  was  asked  to  undergo  further
evaluation/tests, which were to be conducted in the same hospital
on that very day itself. However, the report discloses that after
the first test, when he was supposed to report for the second test,
under the plea of taking some medicines, he went out, but never
returned  and  despite  repeated  calls  being  made,  he  was
incommunicado and was also not responding over phone and the
Medical Board waited till 05:45 p.m. on that day and further
decided to wait for another 24 hours, but still the respondent did
not appear.

--- Page 508 ---
3
8. Pausing here, we note that earlier also, despite repeated
directions issued to the respondent to appear before a Medical
Board for a third assessment, which was upheld by this Court
earlier by order dated 27.10.2025 in Petition(s) for Special Leave
to Appeal (C) No(s).29160 of 2025, the respondent repeatedly did
not appear before the said Board(s) on some pretext or the other. 
9. Coming back to the main issue, the conclusion recorded is: 
“The clinical examination did not reveal any external and
middle  ear  abnormality.  The  audiology  tests  were  not
performed. Hence a conclusive opinion on the status of his
hearing ability and percentage of disability if any cannot
be provided with the existing information. The individual
may be advised to present to the board for completing the
examination procedures for a conclusive opinion.”
10. In  effect,  what  is  stated  is  that  during  the  first
examination, the external ear was normal in both sides, both the
tympanic membranes were normal and intact in both side, bilateral
facial nerve was clinically intact, nose and throat were clinically
normal.
11. Thus, from the conclusion aforesaid, it is clear that the
external and middle ear did not reveal any abnormality, however,
the audiology tests were not performed due to reasons, as indicated
above.
12. Learned  senior  counsel  for  the  respondent  very  humbly
submitted that by way of grace, the Court may consider giving the
respondent one last opportunity for the reason that though, he did
undergo  the  first  test,  but  for  some  unforeseen
circumstances/personal  reasons  he  was  precluded/prevented  from
reporting for the remaining tests.

--- Page 509 ---
4
13. We have considered the matter in its entirety. As the issue
relates to the Civil Services Examination, 2024 conducted by the
appellant no.2-UPSC, taking into account the various reports before
us as also, the conduct of the respondent at Chennai, in our
considered opinion, the matter now requires to be given a quietus.
14. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. The impugned order dated
24.11.2025 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New Delhi in W.P.
(C) 13162 of 2025 is set aside. The appellants are discharged from
the requirement of considering the case of the respondent for
appointment in terms of the Civil Services Examination, 2024 held
by the appellant no.2-UPSC under the category of persons with
disability. 
15. Copy of the report which has been submitted to this Court by
Mr. S.D. Sanjay, learned ASG, be kept on record.
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
          ……………………………………………………………………J.
[AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH]
……………………………………………………………………J.
               [R. MAHADEVAN]
NEW DELHI
MAY 22, 2026

PRIYA SINGH                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
RAGHAV KUMAR SINGH                     Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. The petitioner has filed the Miscellaneous Application  
under Article 142 of the Constitution of India before this Court 
seeking a decree of divorce.
2. During the pendency of aforesaid Miscellaneous Application, 
Mr. Ajit Kumar Sinha, learned Senior Counse l was requested to  
mediate between the parties and the Supreme Court Mediation  
Centre was directed to co-ordinate with the learned Senior  
Counsel to render all necessary assistance  vide  order dated  
24.04.2026. By virtue of the mediation and continued efforts of  
Mr. Ajit Kumar Sinha, the parties have arrived at a settlement  
and  they  have  decided  to  part  ways  on  certain  terms  and 
conditions which are delineated in the settlement agreement  
dated 02.05.2026 which has been signed by both the parties,  
their respective counsel and the Mediator, copy of which has  
been filed.
3. Learned  counsel  for  the  parties  has  filed  a  joint  
1

--- Page 513 ---
application on 25.05.2026 with the following prayers:
“a. Grant a Decree of Divorce dissolving the marriage  
between Applicant No. 1 and Applicant No.2, exercising 
jurisdiction under Article 142 of the Constitution of  
India; and/or
b. Quash all the cases between the parties mentioned  
as per the list given in the Settlement Agreement.
c. Exercise Jurisdiction under Article 142 of the  
Constitution of India read with section 13-B of the  
Hindu Marriage Act and grant divorce by mutual consent 
to  Applicant  No.1  /Applicant  No.1-husband  namely  
Raghav Kumar Singh and Applicant No.2/Applicant No.2-
wife namely Priya Singh.”
4. Further,  the  terms  and  conditions  and  the  relevant  
paragraphs as set out in Settlement Agreement dated 02.05.2026  
are as under: 
“SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT
“1.1 Prior Transactions Acknowledged: The Second Party 
acknowledges receipt of the following amounts and  
articles previously paid or delivered under the First  
Settlement Agreement:
(a) 18,00,000/- (Rupees Eighteen Lakhs Only) paid by  
way of three Demand Drafts on 17.03.2024; and
(b) 50,000/- (Rupees Fifty Thousand Only) transferred₹  
by UPI on 12.02.2024, as per Clause (e) of the First  
Settlement Agreement; and
(c) One gold necklace, two earrings, and one silver  
paijani, handed over by the First Party on 17.03.2024, 
as per Clause (f) of the First Settlement Agreement.
1.2 Settlement Amount: It hereby agreed between the  
Parties that the First Party shall pay to the Second  
Party an amount of 45,00,000/- (Rupees Forty-Five₹  
Lakhs Only) by way of Demand Drafts drawn on a  
nationalised bank in favour of the Second Party, in  
full and final settlement of all financial claims  
between  the  Parties,  including  permanent  alimony, 
maintenance, stridhan, and all claims of any nature  
2

--- Page 514 ---
whatsoever. This amount shall be paid in the following 
manner:
(a) 20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs Only) being the  
balance  settlement  amount  due  under  the  First

Drafts, to be handed over on the date of execution of

(c) 20,80,000/-(Rupees Twenty Lakhs Eighty Thousand

1.3 Upon realisation of the amount referred to in Clause

Demand Drafts are being handed over by the learned counsel for  
the respondent to the learned counsel for the petitioner.
6. We have taken the Joint Application (IA No.139467/2026) and 
Settlement Agreement dated 02.05.2026 on record. 
7. Having considered the matter in detail and having perused  
the Settlement Agreement & Joint Application, which is also  
supported by the affidavits of Ms. Priya Singh, Petitioner and  
Mr. Raghav Kumar Singh, Respondent, we are of the view that this 
is a fit case for exercising power under Article 142 of the  
3

--- Page 515 ---
Constitution of India to dissolve the marriage between the  
parties.  Consequently, the marriage between the parties which  
was solemnized on 01.02.2017 stands dissolved by a decree of

--- Page 518 ---
 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.        OF 2026
(@ Special Leave Petition (Crl.) No. 7100/2025)
YUSUF S            ...APPELLANT(S) 
                   VERSUS
STATE OF KARNATAKA & ORS.              ...RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
2. The  appellant  has  preferred  the  present  appeal  being
aggrieved by the final judgment dated 28.01.2025 passed by the
High Court of Karnataka in Criminal Petition No.9592 of 2023
which  was  filed  by  respondent  Nos.2  to  4  (hereinafter,
collectively referred to as “respondents”) which was allowed by
the High Court and thereby quashed the proceedings arising out
of FIR No.124/2022 dated 19.09.2022 filed at Malleshwaram Police
Station, Bengaluru under Sections 141, 149, 384, 420, 448, 504
and 506 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 (hereinafter referred to as
“IPC”) and Sections 25(1A) of the Arms Act, 1959.
3. Briefly  stated,  the  facts  of  the  case  are  that  the
appellant filed FIR No.124/2022 against the respondents alleging
that:
1

--- Page 519 ---
i. On 12.08.2021, when the appellant was at his home,
the  respondents,  along  with  the  fifty  unknown
persons barged into his house with revolvers, made
obscene  videos  of  him  and  threatened  him  into
entering into a sale agreement with respect to the
property  situated  at  Municipal  No.44/1,  Old
Municipal No.2428, P.I.D. No.8-21-41, Coconut Avenue
Road, Malleshwaram, Bangalore.
ii. The said property was already encumbered with a bank
loan.
iii.The  respondents  forcefully  took  an  amount  of
Rs.1,17,00,000/- and threatened the complainant of
dire consequences if he did not make additional
payment of Rs.67,00,000/-.
4. Aggrieved by initiation of criminal proceedings through FIR
No.124/2022, the respondents preferred the petition seeking
quashing of the same before the High Court in Criminal Petition
No.9592 of 2023.
5. By the impugned order dated 28.01.2025, the High Court
allowed the said Criminal Petition and thereby quashed the
proceedings arising out of the impugned FIR by observing that
upon perusal of the FIR, it was apparent that the necessary
ingredients to constitute commission of offence punishable under
Sections 506, 504, 149, 384, 420, 448 and 141 IPC were not made
out; there was a delay in filing the complaint as the incident
allegedly had occurred on 12.08.2021 whereas the complaint was
2

--- Page 520 ---
filed only on 19.09.2022; the said delay was not satisfactorily
or properly explained by the complainant (the appellant in the
present appeal); and therefore, it was a fit case was made out
for the quashing of proceedings arising out of the impugned FIR
No.124/2022.
6.  Aggrieved  by  the  impugned  order  dated  28.01.2025,  the
appellant has preferred the present appeal.
7. Learned senior counsel Sri Nikhil Goel appearing for the
appellant drew our attention to paragraphs 3 and 4 of the
impugned order dated 28.01.2025 to contend that the said order
is highly cryptic and lacking in reasoning and the petition
seeking quashing of the criminal complaint has been allowed. He
therefore, submitted that only on the said ground, the impugned
order may be set aside and if this Court is so inclined may
remand the matter to the High Court for a reconsideration of
Criminal Petition No.9592 of 2023.  
8. Per contra, learned senior counsel Sri Nuli appearing for
the respondent(s) contended that the material on record would
indicate that this was a fit case where the High Court exercised
its  discretion  justly  in  quashing  the  criminal  complaint.
Although the order may be short, it cannot be said that it is
one without reasoning. He therefore contended that there is no
merit in this appeal.
9. Learned counsel for the respondent-State submitted that
having regard to the arguments advanced by the learned senior
counsel for the respective parties, appropriate orders may be
made in this appeal.
3

--- Page 521 ---
10. We have considered the arguments advanced at the Bar  and
perused the material on record including the impugned order
dated 28.01.2025. 
11. On a perusal of the impugned order, we note that relief has
been granted by the High Court on the basis of what has been
stated in paragraphs 3 and 4 thereof which do not indicate any
reason as to why the criminal petition had to be allowed and the
complaint had to be quashed. We observe that lack of reasoning
in the impugned order while allowing the criminal petition is
sufficient  ground  to  set  aside  the  impugned  order.
Consequently, we set aside the impugned order and restore
Criminal Petition No.9592 of 2023 on the file of the High Court.
12. We  request  the  High  Court  to  consider  the  same  in
accordance with law and on the basis of the material on record
and after hearing learned counsel for the respective parties.
While doing so, we also place reliance upon the observations
made by this Court in Shree Mahavir Carbon Ltd. v. Om Prakash
Jalan, (2015) 12 SCC 653, wherein the Court, while setting aside
the order of the High Court quashing the proceedings arising out
of a complaint case, observed that the principal objective in
giving judgment is to make an effective, practical and workable
decision. It was further observed that while recording the
decision with clarity, the Court is also supposed to record
sufficient reason in taking a particular decision or arriving at
a particular conclusion and the reasons should be such that they
demonstrate  that  the  decision  has  been  arrived  at  on  an
objective consideration.
4

--- Page 522 ---
 It is needless to observe that all contentions on both
sides are left open to be advanced before the High Court. 
The present Appeal is allowed and disposed of in the
aforesaid terms. 
      …………………………………………………J.
[B.V. NAGARATHNA]
…………………………………………………J.
          [UJJAL BHUYAN]
 NEW DELHI
 MAY 20, 2026
5

--- Page 523 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
      CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
Writ Petition(Civil) No. 415 of 2026
TAMILVANI & ORS.                              Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF TAMIL NADU & ORS.                    Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. This Writ Petition is filed under Article 32 of the
Constitution of India seeking the following reliefs:
“a.  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or
direction, more particularly in the nature of
Mandamus, directing the Respondent No.1-State
of Tamil Nadu to forthwith surrender and revert
all unfilled Super Speciality (DM/M.CH) seats
from the State Quota of Tamil Nadu to the All
India Quota, AND
b. Consequently issue an appropriate writ,
order or direction, more particularly in the
nature of Mandamus, directing Respondent No.6
to conduct the Second Round (Round 2) of All
India Counselling for NEET-SS 2025 by including
such reverted seats, strictly on the basis of
the All India Merit List, while ensuring that
all  candidates,  including  those  already
allotted seats in earlier rounds, are permitted
to participate in the said Round 2, so as to
uphold merit and prevent unjust exclusion OR
c. Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or
direction, more particularly in the nature of
Mandamus, directing that, in the alternative
and in the event that the results of Round 2 of
the All India Counselling for NEET-SS 2025 are
declared  prior  to  the  hearing  and  final
1 of 5

--- Page 525 ---
adjudication of the present writ petition, the
Respondents  shall  forthwith  conduct  an
additional round of All India Counselling by
duly  incorporating  all  Super  Speciality
(DM/M.CH) seats reverted from the State Quota
of Tamil Nadu; and
d. Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or
direction, more particularly in the nature of
Mandamus, directing that participation in such
additional round be permitted to all eligible
candidates strictly in accordance with the All
India Merit List, including those candidates
who may have been allotted seats in the earlier
rounds of counselling.
e. Pass such other and further orders as this
Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the
facts and circumstances of the case.”
2. Learned  counsels  for  the  petitioner  and  the
respondents are in agreement that the present writ petition
can  be  disposed  of,  following  the  directions  in  "N.
Karthikeyan & Ors. vs. The State of Tamil Nadu & Ors " in
Writ Petition(C) No. 53 of 2022 dated 02.12.2022, wherein
the following directions were issued:
"However, insofar as the present academic
year is concerned, we find that the State of
Tamil Nadu needs to be permitted to fill in the
post  on  the  basis  of  the  G.O.  No.462  of
07.11.2020. Insofar as the apprehension of the
learned ASG with regard to the seats remaining
vacant  is  concerned,  we  find  that  certain
directions could be issued to ensure that the
seats do not remain vacant. 
We, therefore, permit the State of Tamil
Nadu to fill in the seats reserved on the basis
of  G.O.  No.462  dated  07.11.2020,  within  a
period of 15 days from today. On the 16th day
from today, the State of Tamil Nadu will inform
the Union of India with regard to all the seats
which  remain  unfilled  from  the  in-service
category, which shall be permitted to be filled
in by the Union of India, on the basis of All
India merit list."
 
2 of 5

--- Page 526 ---
3. Mr. S.D. Sanjay, learned Additional Solicitor General
submits that for the present, the cut-off that was existing
on the date of counselling will be maintained unless it
becomes necessary to vary it. 
4. The State of Tamil Nadu will take necessary steps to
intimate the Director General of Health Services about the
151 vacant seats that have remained unfilled. 
5. With  these  directions,  the  Writ  Petition  stands
disposed of.
6. We make it clear that this order is in the facts and
circumstances of the case and shall not be treated as a
precedent. 
      …………………………………………………………………………J.

ITEM NO.28               COURT NO.6               SECTION II-B
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION Diary No. 24243/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  14-09-
2018 in SLP(Crl) No.  8502/2018 14-09-2018 in SLP(Crl) No. No.
8501/2018 passed by the Supreme Court of India]
TARSEM SINGH                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
INTELLIGENCE OFFICER DIRECTORATE OF REVENUE INTELLIGENCE, 
AMRITSARRespondent(s)
IA No. 123129/2026 - APPROPRIATE ORDERS/DIRECTIONS
IA No. 159238/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 123131/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING PAPER BOOKS
IA No. 123114/2026 - RECALLING THE COURTS ORDER
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S.V.N. BHATTI
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Kuldip Singh, AOR (Through VC)
                   Mr. Ajay Bansal, Adv.
                   Ms. Anmol Kaur, Adv.
                   Mr. Gaurav Yadava, Adv.
                   Mr. Sourav Jindal, Adv.
                   Mr. Anurag Yadav, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
1

--- Page 530 ---
M.A.Diary No. 24243/2026
2. The petitioner before us is a convict.  He is undergoing
life imprisonment past more than 25 years. He wants this Court
to look into the order passed by this Court dated 24.04.2024 in
Criminal  Appeal  No.  1056  of  2015  titled  “Harbhajan  Singh
Versus  Intelligence  Officer,  Directorate  Of  Revenue
Intelligence”. The order reads thus:-
“1. By a concurrent judgment, the appellant has been
convicted for the offences punishable under Sections 21,
23  and  29  of  the  Narcotic  Drugs  &  Psychotropic
Substances, Act, 1985 (for short "the NDPS Act') apart
from Section 489-B, 489-C of the Indian Penal Code, 1860,
Section 25 of the Arms Act, 1959 and Sections 4 & 5 of
Explosive Substances Act, 1908. Accordingly, sentence
have been imposed for a period of twenty years for
certain offences while life imprisonment has been imposed
for the offence committed under Section 489-B of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short “the IPC”) and under
Section 4 and 5 of the Explosive Substances Act, 1908.
The High Court confirmed the order of conviction and
sentence passed by the Trial Court making it clear that
sentences shall run consecutively.
2.Though, learned counsel appearing for the appellant
made arguments on merit, we are not inclined to interfere
with  the judgment  of the  High Court  who took  into
consideration all the relevant materials while confirming
the conviction order passed by the Trial Court. 
3.The only other issue for consideration is with respect
to the sentence imposed. For considering the alternative
submission made, we directed the respondent-State to file
2

--- Page 531 ---
M.A.Diary No. 24243/2026
an affidavit on the conduct of the appellant during the
period of incarceration. The appellant has undergone
about a period of 21 years 07 months and 22 days of
actual sentence. The following is the relevant paragraphs
of the affidavit filed on behalf of the respondent:-
“5. That as per information received from
the Jail, the convict has undergone total of
21 years 07 months and 22 days of actual
sentence.
6. That as per information received from
the  Jail,  pertaining  to  the  attending
circumstances, particularly to the conduct of
convict, it is submitted that:
a) The above said convict has been
performing his prison duty which is
assigned to him by prison officials
satisfactory.
b) No case related to prison offence
has been registered against him and
convict  has  never  engaged  in  any
illegal activity inside the prison.
c) The above said convict has availed
total 07 paroles till date and no
adverse  report  during  his  stay  on
parole has ever been received in the
office of Superintendent Central Jail,
Amritsar.
7. That the answering respondent is bound
to  follow  the  directions  passed  by  this
Hon’ble Court in the present Appeal. A copy of
letter No.1644 dated 12.4.2024 received from
the  Superintendent,  Central  Jail,  Amritsar,
Punjab is Annexure A.”
4.We are inclined to take these averments, for coming to
the conclusion that they can be construed as mitigating
circumstances  to  facilitate  modifying  the  sentence
3

--- Page 532 ---
M.A.Diary No. 24243/2026
imposed. The conduct of the appellant during the period
of incarceration has been found to be satisfactory. The
occurrence took place way back in the year 2000. He was
also on parole on seven occasions. There was no adverse
report whenever the appellant was sent on parole. Thus,
making the appellant as a reformed person to get back
into the society, will not cause any problem. 
5.Thus in the light of the aforesaid factual position,
we  are  in clined  to  confirm  the  conviction  while
modifying the sentence passed under aforesaid sections
to one undergone i.e.  21 years 07 months and 22 days of
actual sentence. 
6.We have been informed that there are two more co-
accused similarly placed like that of the appellant. We
believe that the benefit conferred on the appellant will
have to be extended to them provided the report of the
jail authorities is satisfactory as in the case of the
appellant. In such view of the matter, we are inclined to
direct the respondents to give a copy of the order passed
by this Court to the co- accused who can approach this
Court for appropriate direction seeking the same relief
as being rendered to the appellant. 
7.In the event of inability of the co-accused persons to
have legal assistance of their own choice, a request
shall be made to the concerned legal aid Authority of
the State.  
8.The appeal is allowed in part. The appellant shall be
released forthwith, unless required in any other case.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
4

--- Page 533 ---
M.A.Diary No. 24243/2026
of.
 
9. We also place on record the valuable assistance
rendered  by  Ms.  Aishwarya  Bhati,  learned  Additional
Solicitor General. A copy of this Order be sent to the
concerned High Court Legal Services Authority.”
2. In view of the aforesaid, we direct the respondent-State to
file an affidavit on the conduct of the petitioner before us
during the period of 25 years of incarceration before the next
date of hearing.
3. Post this matter on 21.07.2026
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
5

2. Leave granted.
3. The petition filed under Section 9 of the Arbitration and
Conciliation Act, 1996, seeking interim protection from termination
and encashment of Performance Bank Guarantee (“hereinafter referred
to as PBG”) and an ad interim relief to the same effect sought for
which came to be granted on 06.03.2025, till the dismissal on
17.12.2025 is the subject matter of the present appeal.
4. Having heard the learned senior counsels appearing for both the
parties and having regard to the fact that appellant has invoked
the arbitration clause and had approached International Chamber of
Commerce (“for short ICC”) for appointment of Arbitral Tribunal and
same having resulted in Arbitral Tribunal being constituted and the
said Arbitral Tribunal having entered into reference,  the matter
is at large before the said Tribunal, namely all the issues
1

--- Page 550 ---
relating to invocation of the bank guarantee as well as termination
of the Contract is the subject matter of adjudication before the
Arbitral Tribunal.
5. As noticed hereinabove, in the application filed by appellant
herein under Section 9 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act,
1966, the jurisdictional Court had initially granted an interim
relief namely had restrained the respondent(s) herein from invoking
the bank guarantee vide Order dated 06.03.2025 and this order was
in operation till the dismissal of said petition and vacating the
interim order on 17.12.2025. Thereafter, the appeal filed by
appellant under Section 37 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act,
1996, came to be dismissed on 09.01.2026. It is thereafter,
Performance Bank Guarantees were invoked and proceeds of the bank
guarantees  are  realised  by  respondents.  Since  entering  into
adjudication of the rival contentions raised by the learned senior
counsels in this appeal would result in touching upon the merits of
the case which would not be in the best interest of both parties,
since all these issues are pending before the Arbitral Tribunal, we
desist from entering into any adjudication, despite being tempted.
However, in order to balance the equities and in the teeth of the
invocation  of  the  bank  guarantee  having  been  stayed  by  the
jurisdictional Court on 06.03.2025 and it was in operation till
09.01.2026, we deem it proper to direct the respondent(s) to
deposit the proceeds of the bank guarantee which has since been
invoked and received, to be deposited in bank from which it has
been invoked in an Escrow Account. It is needless to state that
2

--- Page 551 ---
Arbitral Tribunal would adjudicate the dispute independently and
would be at liberty to pass such orders as it deems fit with regard
to the payment of the amount in deposit (Escrow Account) and
parties would be governed by same.
6. The appeal stands disposed of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

        IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
      CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(S).              OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.)  No(s).  14482/2025)
TATHYA PRAGNESHBHAI PATEL              APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT            RESPONDENT(S)
       O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant seeks regular bail in connection
with  FIR  being  C.R.No.11191069230241/2023  dated
20.07.2023,  registered  with  S.G.  Highway-02
Traffic Police Station, Ahmedabad City, Gujarat,
for the offences punishable under Sections 279,
337, 338, 304, 504, 506(2) and 114 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860 and under Sections 177, 184 and
134(b) of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.
3. We  have  heard  the  learned  Senior  Counsel
appearing  for  the  appellant  and  the  learned
counsel appearing for the respondent-State.
4. In  sum  and  substance,  the  case  of  the
prosecution is that due to the wilful act of the
appellant  while  driving  a  car,  he  caused  an
accident, which resulted in death of nine persons

--- Page 555 ---
2
and  several  persons  were  injured  due  to  the
alleged occurrence. 
5. Pursuant to the earlier order passed by this
Court on 20.02.2026, whereby, it was directed that
the concerned Trial Court should make an endeavour
to examine the 25 witnesses, who are stated to be
the eye-witnesses in the present case, within a
period of 3 months from the date of receipt of a
copy of the aforesaid order, 29 witnesses have
been examined so far, as has been informed by the
learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  respondent-
State.
6. We  also  take  note  of  the  fact  that  140
witnesses have to be examined before the Trial
Court.
7. Considering  the  period  of  incarceration
undergone by the appellant i.e., about 2 years and
10 months and the stage of trial, we are inclined
to set aside the impugned order and grant bail to
the appellant. However, without prejudice to the
contentions of the appellant and taking note of
the facts governing, we direct the appellant to
deposit a sum of Rs.1 crore with the concerned
Trial Court as soon as possible but not later than
a period of two weeks from the date of receipt of
a copy of this order. Depending upon the final
outcome of the case, the learned Trial Court shall

--- Page 556 ---
3
decide the issue with respect to the disbursement
of the aforesaid amount.
8. Accordingly,  the  impugned  order  stands  set
aside and the appellant is granted bail on the
terms and conditions subject to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.
9. The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
10. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  also
stand disposed of.
………………………………………………………….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]

1. Our Order dated 4-5-2026 reads thus:-
1. By our order dated 27.04.2026, we had called for the

“1. What is the amount due and payable as on date with
interest?

operation?

petitioner to deposit or rather pay an amount of

and final settlement?

Finance Company at the time when loan came to be
sanctioned and what is the amount that the petitioners
paid over a period of years with interest?”
2. In pursuance  of  our  order  referred to  above the  
respondent(s) has filed an affidavit stating as under:-
“SHORT AFFIDAVIT ON BEHALF OF RESPONDENT NO. 1, IIFL HOME
FINANCE LIMITED
(in terms of the Order dated 27.04.2026)
I, Raj, aged about 29 years, son of Mr. Jagat Ram,
working as Legal Manager with the Respondent No. 1,
presently at New Delhi, do hereby solemnly affirm and

1. I am presently working as the Legal Manager with
Respondent No. 1, IIFL Home Finance Limited, having its
