IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
ARBITRATION PETITION NO.47/2025
NEWGEN DIGITALWORKS PVT LTD PETITIONER(S)
                              VERSUS
PETER LANG GROUP AG RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. The instant petition under Section 11(6) of the
Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (Act), seeks the
appointment of a Sole Arbitrator to adjudicate disputes
arising from the Master Services Agreement dated 20.01.2020
(MSA)  and  the  accompanying  Statement  of  Work  dated
20.01.2020 (SOW) executed between the Petitioner and Peter
Lang AG (PLAG), whose liabilities and obligations were
subsequently assumed by the Respondent.
2. Briefly  observed,  the  Petitioner  is  a  company
incorporated under the laws of India and is engaged in the
business  of  providing  end-to-end  publishing,  business
1

--- Page 570 ---
process  outsourcing,  and  IT-enabled  services.  The
Respondent  has  its  registered  office  in  Lausanne,
Switzerland,  and  is  engaged  in  the  publication  of
humanities/social sciences textbooks.
3. Originally,  the  Petitioner’s  Dutch  subsidiary,
Konvertus B.V., was rendering publication services to PLAG.
Owing to PLAG’s sustained financial distress and its entry
into the Swiss moratorium regime in 2019, the Petitioner
stepped in and formalized the arrangement through the MSA
and SOW. Under the MSA, the Petitioner was engaged to
provide end-to-end publishing, quality control, and back-
office functions on a revenue-linked retainer basis. The
SOW defined the scope of work and commercial terms. In July
2020, PLAG underwent a Management Buy-Out (MBO), pursuant
to which its business was transferred to the Respondent
(formerly known as, Peter Lang Copyright AG). To document
the assumption of liabilities, a Debt Transfer Agreement
dated 02.07.2020 (DTA) was executed between the Petitioner,
the Respondent, and PLAG, whereby the Respondent assumed
certain liabilities and obligations of PLAG under the MSA
and SOW.
4. It  appears  that  a  dispute  arose  owing  to  the
Respondent’s alleged failure to pay outstanding dues under
2

--- Page 571 ---
the MSA and SOW, which accumulated to CHF 2,986,128 (Swiss
Francs).
5. Owing to this, the Petitioner issued a Notice of
Arbitration dated 21.11.2024 under Clause 9.2 of the MSA,
nominating Hon’ble Mr. Justice R. V. Easwar, former Judge
of the High Court of Delhi, as the Sole Arbitrator. The
Respondent  declined  to  participate  in  the  appointment
process  and  denied  being  bound  by  the  arbitration
agreement, contending that the MSA and SOW were executed
with PLAG (a distinct entity) and not with the Respondent.
This  prompted  the  Petitioner  to  file  the  instant
Arbitration Petition.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
perused the material placed on record.
7. Keeping in view the facts and circumstances recorded
above and the relevant provision(s) under the MSA, we deem
it  just  and  proper  to  allow  the  instant  Arbitration
Petition and appoint Hon’ble Mr. Justice C. Nagappan, a
former Judge of Supreme Court of India, as the Sole
Arbitrator to adjudicate the disputes between the parties.
The seat and venue of arbitration, in accordance with the
3

--- Page 572 ---
arbitration agreement, shall be Chennai, India. Ordered
accordingly.
8. We make it clear that the parties will be at liberty
to raise all available contentions before the learned Sole
Arbitrator.
9. The learned Sole Arbitrator shall have the liberty to
fix his own remuneration/fees. 
10. Pending  interlocutory  applications,  if  any,  also
stand disposed of.
..........................CJI.
(SURYA KANT)
............................J.
(JOYMALYA BAGCHI)  
     
NEW DELHI;        
MAY 26, 2026

--- Page 580 ---
2
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No(s). 63357/2025
1. Because  of  non-compliance  of  the  order  dated
25.07.2025 in Civil Appeal Nos.9812-9815/2025, the instant
contempt petition has been filed. As alleged by the
contemnors, non-compliance was only due to pendency of
review petition. It is not in dispute that the review
petition  (bearing  R.P.(C)Nos.9319-9322/2026)  has  been
dismissed  vide  order  dated  24.03.2026.  Further,  the
application seeking clarification (M.A.Diary No.3566/2026)
raising similar grounds, filed by the alleged contemnor(s)
has  been  also  been  dismissed  today,  therefore,  with
reluctance, in the interest of justice, we grant further
time of  six weeks time to submit a compliance report
otherwise  Dr.  V.  Kumaresan,  Registrar  of  the  Anna
University, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, would personally remain
present on the next date of hearing.
2. List on 27.07.2026.
Diary No(s). 3566/2026
1. Delay condoned.

--- Page 581 ---
3
2. IA No.142587/2026 for intervention is permitted to
withdrawn as prayed, accordingly, it is dismissed as
withdrawn.
3. These  miscellaneous  applications  (D.No.3566/2026)
have been filed seeking clarification of the order of this
Court dated 25.07.2025 passed in Civil Appeal Nos.9812-
9815/2025.
4. It is not in dispute that previously, the review
petitions were filed (R.P.(C)Nos.9319-9322/2026) against
our order dated 25.07.2025, and the same were dismissed on
24.03.2026. It is also mentioned by the learned counsel
for the applicants that the curative petition(s) has also
filed against the dismissal of the said review petitions
which is presently pending in defects.
5. Now,  raising  similar  grounds  on  merits  this
application(s) seeking clarification has been filed.
6. In  our  view,  such  application(s)  is  not
entertainable.  It  is  to  observe  that,  the  issue  of
maintainability  has  been  decided  by  the  reportable

--- Page 582 ---
4
judgment in Miscellaneous Application No.1276/2026 titled
as “Fakir Mamad Suleman Sameja & Ors. Vs. Adani Ports And
Special  Economic  Zone  Ltd  &  Ors.”,  pronounced  on
12.05.2026 (2026 INSC 483), wherein this Court clarified
about the jurisdiction in miscellaneous application after
disposal. It is clear that the scope to entertain such an
application is limited and it is not permissible to re-
agitate the merits after the case has reached its outcome
on similar grounds which have been rejected by the Court
in the review petition.
7. Accordingly, the application seeking clarification
stands dismissed.
8. Miscellaneous  applications  are  disposed  of
accordingly.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (NAND KISHOR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        BRANCH OFFICER

--- Page 591 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 2851 OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 785 OF 2026)
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND                             APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
SUGRIV PURAN                                      RESPONDENT
O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellant-State of Jharkhand is aggrieved by the
grant of bail to the respondent by the High Court, who
upon conviction, has been awarded sentence of 10 years
rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs.1,00,000/- (Rupees
One  Lakh)  vide order  dated  04.02.2025  passed  by  the
Additional Sessions Judge cum Special Judge (NDPS Act),
Seraikela Kharsawan. In the appeal filed by him before the
High Court, the sentence has been suspended and he has
been released on bail after being in custody for only 2
years and 2 months.
4. Learned counsel for the appellant-State of Jharkhand
submitted that upon receiving information that the vehicle
of the respondent was carrying narcotics, and when the
police reached the site, upon seeing the Police, the
driver  of  vehicle  fled  away  and  managed  to  escape.
However, on search of the vehicle, 177 kgs of Poppy Straw
was recovered. It was contended that there is no other co-
1

--- Page 592 ---
accused and the respondent being the owner of the vehicle,
was found to be the person, who was dealing in such
contraband item and was sent for trial, which resulted in
his  conviction.  It  was  contended  that  the  admitted
position is that the respondent was the owner of the
vehicle from which recovery has been made, and further
that no other person was either named or was found to be
complicit in the crime during the course of investigation.
Learned counsel further submitted that in such a case when
minimum sentence of 10 years has been awarded and the
respondent has undergone only 2 years and 2 months of
incarceration, which is not even 1/4 th of the awarded
sentence, his release on bail by the High Court was not
proper.
5. Learned counsel for the respondent submitted that he
was not caught at the spot and that the vehicle was a
commercial vehicle, which he had given on rent to someone
else and thus, he was not responsible for the crime. It
was further submitted that he has no criminal antecedents
and was arrested after six months of the incident.
6. On a query of the Court as to why the respondent
waited for six months before moving for release of his
vehicle, his stand is that he was trying to pursue the
matter with the police, but the police was influenced by
the real culprit. On a further query as to who the real
culprit was, there was no answer forthcoming. We find such
response to be totally unsatisfactory and unbelievable.
7. Be that as it may, we agree with the submissions of
2

--- Page 593 ---
learned counsel for the appellant-State of Jharkhand that
the High Court did not properly exercise its discretion
for suspension of sentence and grant of bail to the
respondent in the present case.
8. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. The impugned order
stands set aside.
9. The respondent is directed to surrender before the
Trial Court latest within two weeks from today.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.  
....................J.
(AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH)
....................J.
(R. MAHADEVAN)
NEW DELHI
MAY 26, 2026
3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION

 SANGEETA KUMARI              PETITIONER(s)

 UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                         RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Pursuant to our direction, the Under Secretary,
Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, Government of
India, New Delhi has filed an affidavit, relevant
portion which is as follows:
“30.  That  the  Ministry  of  Health  &
Family  Welfare  is  presently  taking
necessary administrative and financial
measures for augmentation and allocation
of funds from other available sources so
as to facilitate release of compensation
in eligible pending cases including the
case of the present Petitioner.
31.  That  the  answering  Respondent
remains committed to honouring genuine
and  verified  claims  under  the  PMGKP
Scheme and has no intention whatsoever
to deny lawful entitlement to eligible
beneficiaries.
32.  That  upon  availability  and
allocation of requisite funds under the
appropriate  budgetary  head,  the
compensation amount of Rs. 50,00,000/-
(Rupees  Fifty  Lakhs  Only)  shall  be
transferred directly to the Petitioner
in  accordance  with  the  prescribed
procedure.
33. That in the aforesaid facts and
circumstances, the answering Respondent
most respectfully seeks a period of 8
weeks  from  this  Hon'ble  Court  for
completion  of  the  necessary
1

--- Page 608 ---
administrative and financial formalities
for release of payment.
34.  In  view  of  the  facts  and
circumstances stated hereinabove, it is
most  respectfully  prayed  that  this
Hon'ble Court may graciously be pleased
to grant 8 weeks period to the answering
Respondent for arranging and releasing
compensation payable to the Petitioner
under the PMGKP Scheme.”
2. It is evident from the above that the petitioner’s
claim is found fit and the respondent seeks eight weeks
time for releasing the payment. The time sought for is
granted.
3. The Writ Petition is disposed of recording the
undertaking  in  Paragraph  No.34  of  the  affidavit
extracted herein above.
4. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
Writ Petition(s)(Civil)  No(s).  274/2025
 SANGEETA KUMARI              PETITIONER(s)
                                VERSUS
 UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                         RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Pursuant to our direction, the Under Secretary,
Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, Government of
India, New Delhi has filed an affidavit, relevant
portion which is as follows:
“30.  That  the  Ministry  of  Health  &
Family  Welfare  is  presently  taking
necessary administrative and financial
measures for augmentation and allocation
of funds from other available sources so
as to facilitate release of compensation
in eligible pending cases including the
case of the present Petitioner.
31.  That  the  answering  Respondent
remains committed to honouring genuine
and  verified  claims  under  the  PMGKP
Scheme and has no intention whatsoever
to deny lawful entitlement to eligible
beneficiaries.
32.  That  upon  availability  and
allocation of requisite funds under the
appropriate  budgetary  head,  the
compensation amount of Rs. 50,00,000/-
(Rupees  Fifty  Lakhs  Only)  shall  be
transferred directly to the Petitioner
in  accordance  with  the  prescribed
procedure.
33. That in the aforesaid facts and
circumstances, the answering Respondent
most respectfully seeks a period of 8
weeks  from  this  Hon'ble  Court  for
completion  of  the  necessary
1

--- Page 624 ---
administrative and financial formalities
for release of payment.
34.  In  view  of  the  facts  and
circumstances stated hereinabove, it is
most  respectfully  prayed  that  this
Hon'ble Court may graciously be pleased
to grant 8 weeks period to the answering
Respondent for arranging and releasing
compensation payable to the Petitioner
under the PMGKP Scheme.”
2. It is evident from the above that the petitioner’s
claim is found fit and the respondent seeks eight weeks
time for releasing the payment. The time sought for is
granted.
3. The Writ Petition is disposed of recording the
undertaking  in  Paragraph  No.34  of  the  affidavit
extracted herein above.
4. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

--- Page 667 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL)  NO.2209/2025
JYOTSNARANI HATI                              Petitioner(s)
                          VERSUS
LAKSHAY JINDAL                                Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The parties have also appeared through virtual mode.
3. Through this petition, the petitioner – wife seeks
transfer of proceedings pending as HMA No.83/2025 pending
before Principal Judge, Family Court, Kurukshetra, Haryana
to a court of competent jurisdiction Family Court, Jajpur,
Odisha.
4. Vide order dated 24.09.2025, at the request of learned
counsel for the parties, the matter was sent to the
Mediation Centre to explore a settlement. The Mediation
Centre  of  this  Court  has  submitted  a  report  dated
23.02.2026 annexing therewith a Settlement Agreement dated
21.02.2026 arrived between the parties.
1

--- Page 668 ---
5. In  terms  of  the  Settlement  Agreement  a  sum  of
Rs.15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs) was to be paid by the
second  party  (the  respondent)  towards  full  and  final
settlement of all claims arising out of the matrimonial
relationship between the parties. The petitioner accepts
that the aforesaid amount has already been paid to her.
The  settlement  agreement  also  provide  for  return  of
jewellery articles which parties agree to abide.
6. In such circumstances, the learned counsel for the
parties have prayed that in terms of paragraph 5 of the
Settlement Agreement a decree of divorce by mutual consent
be passed by this Court.
7. Having regard to the fact that parties have arrived at
an amicable settlement and they are willing to part ways by
mutual consent, we are of the view that it is a fit case
for invocation of our jurisdiction under Article 142 of the
Constitution of India to dissolve the marriage by mutual
consent in terms of the settlement.
8. Accordingly, this transfer petition stands disposed of
in terms of the settlement agreement.  
9. Let a decree of dissolution of marriage be drawn in
the aforesaid terms. The Settlement Agreement shall be part
of the decree.
2

--- Page 669 ---
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………………………J
[MANOJ MISRA]
…………………………………………………………………J
[MANMOHAN]
New Delhi 
March 19, 2026
3

--- Page 670 ---

                   Mr. Kartikey Bhatt, Adv.
                   Mr. Prashant Alai, Adv.                 
                                      
                   Mr. Siddharth Aggarwal, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Anusuya Sadhu Sinha, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Naidu, Adv.
                   Mr. Pratim Dasgupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Saurabh Bharadwaj, Adv.
                   Ms. Aditi Sharma, Adv.
    For M/S.  KSM & Co., AoR
                                      
O R D E R
Permission to file the SLP(Crl.) Diary No.62514 of 2025 is
granted.
2. Heard Mr. Siddharth Dave, learned senior counsel for the
petitioner, Mr. Siddharth Aggarwal, learned senior counsel for the
respondent-Jayant Singh and Mr. Kunal Mimani, learned Advocate-on-
Record for the respondent-State of West Bengal.
3. The present petitions have been filed against the impugned
order dated 09.10.2025 passed by the High Court of Calcutta in
C.R.M.(M) 1923 of 2025 for cancellation of bail granted to the
respondent-Jayant Singh.
4. The brief facts of the case are that a charge sheet has been
submitted against the respondent-Jayant Singh under Section 111 of
the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (hereinafter referred to as the
“BNS, 2023”) in a case arising out of FIR No.91 of 2024 dated
03.07.2024  on one hand , whereas on the other hand, in a case
arising out of FIR No.267 of 2024 dated 01.07.2024, a similar

--- Page 681 ---
3
charge sheet has been submitted under the same Section 111 of the
BNS, 2023. The similarity between the two is that in the present
case, the submission of the charge sheet is based on thirteen
criminal antecedents against the respondent-Jayant Singh whereas,
in the other FIR, it is based on sixteen criminal antecedents,
including the thirteen in the present case.
5. Though,  learned  senior  counsel  for  the  petitioner  has
indicated that in the impugned order, there is a reference that the
respondent-Jayant Singh is in bail in all other cases filed against
him which is factually incorrect. 
6. On  a  quick  response,  learned  senior  counsel  for  the
respondent-Jayant Singh submits that since in the present case
i.e.,  FIR  No.91  of  2024,  with  regard  to  thirteen  criminal
antecedents cited, in all those cases, he is on bail and though, he
may not be on bail on the remaining three cases which are included
in the charge sheet under Section 111 of the BNS, 2023 in the other
case, i.e., in FIR No.267 of 2024 that was the reason why it was
represented before the Court that he was on bail in all those
cases, and thus, there was no suppression by the respondent no.2.
7. Having  considered  the  matter,  we  are  not  persuaded  to
interfere in the impugned order(s). Accordingly, the Special Leave
Petitions are dismissed.
8. However, the Court in the facts and circumstances of the
present case and taking into consideration the criminal antecedents
would request the High Court to decide the case in which, bail has
been granted to the respondent-Jayant Singh in a case against
which, he has filed for quashing before the High Court, is taken up

--- Page 682 ---
4
and decided expeditiously.
9. Parties may bring the present order to the notice of, either
the Bench before whom the case may be listed and if not listed,
before the Hon’ble Chief Justice of High Court at Calcutta.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 691 ---
THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
ARBITRATION PETITION NO.1/2026
EASY HANDLING LLC PETITIONER(S)
                              VERSUS
PRADHAAN AIR EXPRESS PRIVATE LIMITED RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. The instant petition under Section 11(6) r/w Section
11(12)(a) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
(1996 Act), seeks the appointment of a 3-member Arbitral
Tribunal to adjudicate the disputes between the parties
arising out of the Aircraft Charter Agreement (Charter
Agreement) dated 08.03.2024.
2. For context, the Petitioner is a company incorporated
in Georgia and provides logistic support to its end-
customers. The Respondent is a company registered under
the Companies Act, 1956 and provides chartered aircraft
for the transportation of cargo. The parties entered into
an Aircraft Charter Agreement dated 08.03.2024, whereby
the  Respondent  would  provide  dedicated  air  freight
services to the Petitioner. The Respondent was obligated
to operate 25 rotations a month (approx. 6 rotations per
week) for a price of USD 20,000 per rotation.
3. It is claimed that disputes arose owing to the
Respondent’s inability to provide the required number of
rotations per week. Aggrieved, the Petitioner sent a
1

--- Page 692 ---
Termination Notice dated 19.07.2024 under Clause 16 of
the  Charter  Agreement,  seeking  a  refund  of  USD
103,398.65, which is stated to be the advance payment
made  for  which  no  services  were  rendered,  and  the
security deposit of USD 50,000. 
4. Since no response was received from the Respondent,
the  Petitioner  issued  a  Notice  Invoking  Arbitration
dated  18.01.2025  under  Clause  21  of  the  Charter
Agreement,  and  nominated  Hon’ble  Mr.  Justice  Jayant
Nath,  Former  Judge,  High  Court  of  Delhi  as  its
Arbitrator.  As  the  Respondent  did  not  nominate  an
Arbitrator or reply to the said notice, the instant
petition was filed.
5. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
perused the material on record.
6. Keeping in view the facts and circumstances recorded
above, we deem it just and proper to allow the instant
Arbitration Petition and appoint Hon’ble Mr. Justice
Chander Shekhar, former Judge of Delhi High Court, as
the Arbitrator on behalf of the Respondent. Further, we
direct the two Arbitrators, one being already appointed
by the Petitioner, to appoint a Presiding Arbitrator in
accordance with Clause 21 of the Charter Agreement. The
seat and venue of arbitration shall be New Delhi, India.
Ordered accordingly.
7. We make it clear that the parties will be at liberty
to raise all available contentions before the learned
2

--- Page 693 ---
Arbitral Tribunal.
8. The Arbitral Tribunal shall have the liberty to fix
its own remuneration/fees. 
9. Pending interlocutory applications, if any, also
stand disposed of.
..........................CJI.
(SURYA KANT)
............................J.
(JOYMALYA BAGCHI)  
     
NEW DELHI;        
MAY 26, 2026
3

--- Page 694 ---

--- Page 705 ---
Upon hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                               O R D E R
1. The petitioner challenges the judgment and order dated
14.06.2023 passed in Criminal Petition No.100212/2023 (439)
by the High Court of Karnataka, Circuit Bench at Dharwad.
2. Delay condoned.
3. We have heard the learned counsel for the petitioner.
4. Having considered the matter in its entirety and gone
through the material on record, we are not inclined to
grant bail to the petitioner, though he may have been under
incarceration for a period of more than five (05) years.
5. Accordingly,  the  Special  Leave  Petition  stands
dismissed.
6. However, in view of what has been contended by learned
counsel for the petitioner that the injured person has been
evading his examination before the Court for the last one
and a half years, we find that the State of Karnataka has
been slack in its duty towards the system by ensuring that
witnesses are produced on time and the trial is concluded
at  the  earliest,  more  so,  when  the  accused  is  in
incarceration.
7. Accordingly, we direct the State of Karnataka to ensure
that the said victim is examined before the Court below
within a period of three (03) weeks from today. The Trial
Court shall also ensure the same.
8. The Registry is directed to transmit a copy of this
order to the Superintendent of Police, District Koppal,
2

--- Page 706 ---
Karnataka forthwith.
9. Needless to say that if, thereafter, an application for
bail  is  filed  by  the  petitioner,  the  same  shall  be
considered on its own merits in accordance with law.
10.  Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  also  stand
disposed of.

was granted anticipatory bail by the Trial Court in connection
with Crime No. 285 of 2025 registered with Khudel Police Station
for the offence punishable under Sections 108 and 85 of the
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, respectively (for short, “the
BNS, 2023).
3. The case of the prosecution against the petitioner is one of
abetment of suicide. It appears that the wife of the petitioner
hanged herself to death, leaving behind two suicide notes. The
Respondent No.2 being dissatisfied and aggrieved by grant of
anticipatory  bail,  went  before  the  High  Court  to  get  it
cancelled.  The High Court by its impugned order remitted the
matter to the Trial Court for fresh consideration of the plea
for anticipatory bail.

inclined to disturb the impugned order passed by the High Court.

disposed of.

Heard Mr. Aman Panwar, learned AAG for the petitioner-
State of Karnataka and Mr. Nachiketa Joshi, learned Senior
Counsel for the respondents. 
2. In response to our previous order dated 02.04.2026,
learned counsel for the Petitioner-State of Karnataka
submits that the issue in the present case is with regard
to whether the intermediary can be held responsible/liable
for the wrong done by the principal during the course of
sale of the principal’s merchandise, through the platform
of the intermediary. This question was before the High
Court, which, in the impugned order, relied upon another
judgment in similar circumstances, where the order in
favour of the intermediary passed by the High Court was
challenged before this Court, but was not entertained.
However, he submits that the Court, while dismissing the
said Special Leave Petitions being SLP(Criminal) Nos.
7454-7455  of  2021,  vide order  dated  20.10.2021,  had
categorically noted that this Court was not giving any
imprimatur to the observations made in the impugned order
on various legal propositions.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner-State of Karnatka
further  submitted  that  there  are  serious  errors  in
consideration of the matter by the High Court and a
2

--- Page 727 ---

Special Leave Petition on behalf of the State of Karnataka
in the present matter, is in the process of being filed

O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. Pursuant to our order dated 27.05.2026, the respondent
has deposited a sum of Rs.4,00,000/- (Rupees Four Lakhs)
before the Trial Court, which according to learned counsel
for the respondent is the mode by which the respondent has
been depositing the amount in the past also. It was
submitted that thereafter, the petitioner used to withdraw
the amount from the Trial Court.
3. Learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner  submits  that
payment has been made except for the months of March, 2025
and April, 2025.
4. Learned counsel for the respondent undertakes that the
payment for the months of March, 2025 and April, 2025,
shall  also  be  made  within  two  weeks  from  today  and
1

--- Page 732 ---
CONMT.PET.(C) NO.173/2026
thereafter, up-to-date payment shall be made in terms of
the previous order(s), if not already paid. 
5. Be that as it may, since the matter relates to
contempt of an order of this Court, which according to the
records and the admitted position indicates, has been
complied with, except for payment for March, 2025 and
April, 2025, which has been undertaken to be paid within
two weeks, we find that the Contempt has been purged.
However, we indicate that payment in terms of the order of
the High Court dated 16.10.2023 in Criminal Revision No.
4090 of 2023 shall continue to be made by the respondent
on time. Needless to state that the same shall be subject
to the final decision in the main case, which is pending
consideration before the High Court.
6. The  Contempt  Petition  stands  disposed  of  in  the
aforementioned terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

Heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
2. Leave granted.
3. Despite service of notice, nobody appears on behalf of
the respondent-husband.
4. The  appellant  is  aggrieved  by  the  order  dated
21.06.2024 passed by the High Court of Orissa at Cuttack
in Trp.Crl.No.145 of 2024, whereby the High Court has
dismissed the petition filed by the appellant-wife seeking
transfer of divorce proceedings from the Family Court,
Bhubaneswar to the Family Court at Cuttack.
5. Having regard to the facts and circumstances of the
case and after hearing learned counsel for the appellant-
wife, we are inclined to allow this appeal.
6.  Accordingly,  the  appeal  is  allowed  and  the  order
impugned passed by the High Court is set aside.
7. The proceedings in C.P. No. 481 of 2021 is directed to
be  transferred  from  the  Family  Court-II,  Bhubaneswar,
Orissa to the Family Court at Cuttack, Orissa.
1

--- Page 735 ---
8. After the matter is transferred to the Family Court at
Cuttack,  Orissa,  the  concerned  Court  will  give  fresh
notice to the parties by fixing a date and the aforesaid
matter shall proceed before the said Court from the stage
it was before the Family Court II, Bhubaneswar, Orissa.
9.  The  respondent-husband  is  at  liberty  to  move  an
application before the transferee court seeking permission
for his presence through virtual mode, which shall be
considered  in  accordance  with  law,  depending  on  the
infrastructure and facilities available at that Court.
10.  The  records  of  the  case  be  transferred  to  the
transferee Court forthwith.
11.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SLP (C) NO.10813/2026]
C. CHANDRAGUPTA & ANR.                        Appellant(s)
VERSUS
R.V. SARAVANAMUNI & ORS.                     Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The limited issue involved in this appeal is whether
the order of remand can be made to the office of District
Revenue Officer instead of Revenue Divisional Officer.
3. In our considered view, the order of remand passed by
the High Court cannot be faulted, except to the extent that
the matter  ought to have been remitted to the District
Revenue Officer rather than the Revenue Divisional Officer.
4. In such view of the matter, the impugned order stands
modified only to the extent that the remand shall be made
to the office of District Revenue Officer instead of the
Revenue Divisional Officer.
5. Considering the facts of the case, the  District
Revenue Officer is directed to pass appropriate final order
within a period of three months from the date of receipt of
this order.

--- Page 744 ---
SLP (C) NO.10813/2026
6. In the meanwhile, the status quo granted earlier
shall continue.
7. Accordingly,  the appeal  is  disposed  of  in  the
aforesaid terms.
8.  Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

parties and attested by the learned counsel appearing on both
sides, dated 10.04.2026, to the effect that the matter has been
resolved between the parties. The memorandum of settlement
that has been duly arrived at between the parties shall form a
part of this order.

Lakh only) has been handed over in the Court in favour of the
petitioner-wife through her counsel, a copy of which is placed on
the file. It is also not in dispute that the remaining part of the
amount has already been received by her. The following pending
case shall be closed/withdrawn:-

1

--- Page 747 ---

the  Constitution  of  India,  1950,  there  shall  be  a  mutual
divorce between the parties, as agreed upon.

respondent shall be drawn a ccordingly. As indicated in the
agreement, the parties shall have no claim against each other
in future.
A  copy of th is order shall be sent to the respective
Courts for being plac ed on  the files of the respective cases
and for consigning the same to record.
The Transfer Petition is, accordingly, disposed of.

of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
I.A. No.147668 of 2025
IN
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO.1211 OF 2025
IN
     CIVIL APPEAL NO.7057 OF 2025
            
    
MAIMMONAMMAL ARIF & ORS.                 APPLICANTS/PETITIONERS
P1 : MAIMMONAMMAL ARIF
P2 : ANFAL ARIF
P3 : FATHIMA BEEVI
                                VERSUS
PONNAN M. A. & ANR.                                    RESPONDENTS
                                
R1 : PONNAN M. A.
R2 : THE DIVISIONAL MANAGER, ORIENTAL INSURANCE
CO. LTD.
O R D E R
Heard learned senior counsel/counsel for the parties.
2. The present application has been filed seeking clarification
of the order dated 21.05.2025 passed by this Court in Civil Appeal
No.7057 of 2025. 
3. Learned senior counsel for the applicants-petitioners submits
that in the said order at paragraph no.7, though it has been noted

--- Page 786 ---
2
that a sum of Rs.41,21,664/- (Rupees Forty One Lakhs Twenty One
Thousand Six Hundred Sixty Four) along with the interest of 8% per
annum  was  to  be  paid  by  the  respondent  no.2  to  the
appellants/applicants, it has further been noted that the same be
done within a period of two months from that day, “after adjusting
what may already have been paid.” It was submitted that as on that
day, both the parties had worked out a calculation, the aforesaid
amount was over and above what had already been paid by that day
and thus, the present observation that “after adjusting what may
already have been paid” has created a confusion, which the Court
may clarify/remove.
4. Learned counsel for the respondent no.2-Insurance Company does
not dispute the said position.
5. Accordingly, the words “after adjusting what may already have
been paid” in paragraph no.7 of the order dated 21.05.2025, stand
deleted. Rest of the order remains the same.
6. Accordingly, the Interlocutory Application No.147668 of 2025
stands allowed in the aforementioned terms.
7. Consequently, the Miscellaneous Application stands disposed
of.
          ……………………..………………………………………………J.
[AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH]
…………………………………………………………………………J.
               [R. MAHADEVAN]
NEW DELHI
MAY 26, 2026

--- Page 787 ---
3

ITEM NO.37               COURT NO.6               SECTION X
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Writ Petition(s)(Civil) No(s). 125/2025
MEHBOOB REHAN                                      Petitioner(s)
                             VERSUS
GOVERNMENT MEDICAL COLLEGE, BETTIAH & ORS.         Respondent(s)
(IA No. 34874/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 34873/2025 - STAY APPLICATION)
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s): Mr. Mayank Sapra, AOR
                   Ms. Lalima Das, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhi Sharma, Adv. 
  
For Respondent(s): Mr. Azmat Hayat Amanullah, AOR
                   Ms. Rebecca Mishra, Adv. 
  
                   Mr. Atul Jha, Adv.
                   Ms. Shruti Jose, Adv.
                   Mr. Sandeep Kumar Jha, AOR 
  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner before us is a successful NEET-UG 2024  
candidate claiming benchmark locomotor disability of Muscular  
Dystrophy in the lower limbs to the tune of 58%. He was  
recommended for allotment of a seat in 1st Year, M.B.B.S.  
under Disability Quota in the Respondent No. 1 college.  
2.  It  is  evident  from  record  that  two  months  after  
1

--- Page 798 ---
petitioner’s enrolment, on verification of record, respondent  
No. 1 issued an Office Order dated 19.12.2024, directing some  
students,  including  the  petitioner,  to  submit  a  fresh  
Disability Certificate through respondent No. 2 hospital. The  
hospital  constituted  a  Medical  Board  on  02.01.2025  for  
examining  four  candidates  including  the  petitioner.  After 
detailed examination, the Government Medical College, Bettiah,  
West  Champaran  issued  a  letter  dated  24.01.2025  holding  
petitioner is ineligible for pursuing the medical course. The  
relevant portion of the order is as follows:
“Subject:  Regarding  the  notification  of  
disqualification  based  on  disability  
verification as per IGIMS, Sheikhpura, Patna-
14, Letter No.-54, dated 10.01.2025.
You are hereby informed that through Principal, 
Government Medical College, Bettiah’s Letter  
No.-1892, dated 19.12.2024, you were instructed 
to  undergo  verification  of  your  disability 
status at IGMIS, Sheikhpura, Patna-14, within  
seven days, where you appeared and underwent  
the  required  medical  examination.  In  the  
meeting  of  the  Medical  Board,  it  was  
unanimously decided that you have been declared 
ineligible for the medical course. In light of  
this decision, you are directed to appear on  
10.02.2025 (Monday) at 11:00 am to confirm your 
consent. Failing to do so shall result in the  
cancellation of your admission.”
3. The petitioner challenged the abovesaid order by filing  
the present writ petition under Article 32 of the Constitution  
of India. While issuing notice, this Court by its order dated  
10.02.2025 directed that the admission of the petitioner shall  
not be disturbed pending disposal of the writ petition.
4. Heard  learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner  and  the  
2

--- Page 799 ---
respondents. Learned counsel for the respondent(s) agreed that  
a disputed question about the eligibility of the petitioner  
can be re-examined following the decision of this Court in the  
case of Om Rathod v. The Director General of Health Services  
and Others, [2024 SCC OnLine 5]. In view of the suggestion of  
Mr. Azmat Hayat Amanullah, learned counsel for the respondent,  
we direct the Director of All India Institute of Medical  
Sciences (AIIMS), Patna to constitute a team of three doctors  
and examine the petitioner and submit a report to this Court  
within a period of eight weeks from today. The petitioner will  
be informed about the date of examination within a period of  
three weeks from today.
5. The report of AIIMS, Patna shall be submitted to the  
Registry of this Court. The Registry is directed to forward a  
copy of our order to the Director, AIIMS, Patna.
6. List on 24.08.2026.
(ABHINAV KUMAR)                                 (NIDHI WASON)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

ITEM NO.3               COURT NO.3               SECTION X
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION Diary No(s). 162/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  09-12-2024
in D No. No. 55052/2024 passed by the Supreme Court of India]
K. BALAKRISHNAN                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THIRU R. KIRIOSH KUMAR & ORS.                      Respondent(s)
IA No. 521/2025 - APPEAL AGAINST REGISTRARS ORDER XV RULE 5
IA No. 154692/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 154691/2026 - PERMISSION TO APPEAR AND ARGUE IN PERSON
IA No. 154695/2026 - RESTORATION
 
Date : 01-06-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
For Petitioner(s) :  Petitioner-in-person
                    
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The  present  Miscellaneous  Application  and  the
interlocutory applications are in the nature of appeal against
the  order  dated  09.12.2024  passed  by  the  Registrar  (J-A)
lodging the Writ Petition (Civil) D. No. 55052 of 2024. The
applicant/petitioner  seeking  reconsideration/review  of  order
dated 01.10.2024 passed in Curative Petition (C) No. 177 of
2024 which came to be dismissed on the ground of delay. 
3. By a detailed order dated 09.12.2024, the Registrar refused
1 of 2

--- Page 847 ---
to receive and register the Writ Petition and proceeded to
lodge it under Order XV Rule 5 of the Supreme Court Rules.
4. Having considered the matter in detail, we are of the
opinion that there was absolutely no justification in filing
Writ Petition under Article 32 of the Constitution of India,
particularly when the very same issue was considered in the
Curative Petition after long drawn litigation commencing from
2019. 
5. We are of the opinion that the decision of the Registrar to
lodge the Writ Petition is correct in law. The Miscellaneous
Application  along  with  interlocutory  applications  are
accordingly dismissed  with costs quantified at Rs 25,000/-
(Rupees Twenty Five Thousand). The amount shall be deposited
with  the  “Supreme  Court  Advocate-on-Record  Association
(SCAORA)” within three weeks from today.
(KAPIL TANDON)                                  (NIDHI WASON)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
2 of 2

--- Page 848 ---

at Ernakulam.

respondent-State is waived. Let notice be issued to the remaining
respondents.
1

--- Page 883 ---
4. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted. Let steps for
service be taken within one week.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioner(s) shall serve a copy of
the petition to the learned standing counsel for the State of
Kerala within a period of one week,
6. In the notice itself, let it be mentioned that respondents are
required  to  file  their  counter  affidavit  and  reply  to  the
interlocutory application(s), if any, before the next date of
listing.

four weeks.  Rejoinder thereto, if any,be filed within two weeks
thereafter.

pending before the judicial First Class Magistrate Court-IV,
Thiruvananthapuram, pursuant to the order dated  23.02.2026 in

by the High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad]
YUSUF JIVAN PATEL @ MAHENDRABHAIJIVANBHAIVASAVA    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT & ANR.                            Respondent(s)
(IA No. 164013/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING, IA No.
164018/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE
DEFECTS AND IA No. 164017/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE
IMPUGNED JUDGMENT)
 
Date : 01-06-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KAROL
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH
           (PARTIAL COURT WORKING DAYS BENCH)
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Mohd Parvez Dabas, Adv.
                   Mr. Syed Mehdi Imam, AOR
                   Mr. Mohd Noorullah, Adv.
                   Mr. Uzmi Jamil Husain, Adv.
                   Ms. Syeda Alia Fatema, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s) :Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, AOR 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner challenges the judgment and order dated
01.10.2025  in  CRMA  No.13667/2022  and  30-09-2025  in  CRMA
No.13667/2022 titled “Varyava Abdul Vahab Mahmood vs. State of
Gujarat  &  Anr.”  passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Gujarat  at
Ahmedabad.
2. Delay condoned.
1

--- Page 885 ---
3. Having heard learned counsel for the parties, we are of
the considered view that interest of justice would be best met
with the disposal of the present petition on the following
terms:
(A) Since the investigation is complete and charge
sheet stands filed, it shall be open for the instant
petitioner to raise all pleas, subject matter of the
lis, before the appropriate Court at the time of framing
of charges for the purpose of seeking discharge.
(B) Since the FIR pertains to the year 2021, we direct
the trial be expedited.  
(C) As and when accused files an application seeking
discharge,  the  same  shall  be  heard  and  decided  on
expeditious  basis,  uninfluenced  by  any  of  the
observations made in the impugned order.
(D) Needless to add, all concerned i.e.the prosecution
and the accused shall fully co-operate and not take any
unnecessary adjournment(s).
4. With the above observations, special leave petition stands
disposed of.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.
(RAJNI MUKHI)                                   (ANU BHALLA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

                   Ms. Talat Chaudhary, Adv.                   
                   
           UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                        O R D E R
Delay condoned.
Having  considered  the  matter,  we  are  not
inclined to entertain this petition.  Accordingly,
the Special Leave Petition is dismissed.
1

--- Page 895 ---
The High Court vide order dated 20 th December,
2025 has granted six months’ time  to the petitioner
to  vacate the premises in question till 20 th June,
2026. It has also  also been brought to our notice
that the monthly rent being paid by the petitioner
is  275/- (Rupees Two Hundred  and Seventy  Five₹
Only), which was fixed in the year 1978.
Considering  the  aforesaid  facts  and
circumstances,  we deem it appropriate to extend the
time for vacating the premises until  30 th June,
2026, only subject to the petitioner’s filing the
usual undertaking  in  this  regard  before  this
Court as well as  before the Trial Court  within  one
week from today.  The  petitioner  shall  handover
vacant and peaceful possession of the premises to the
respondent   on  or  before  30th June,  2026.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
(SONIA BHASIN)

(RANJANA SHAILEY)

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R

very  fairly  pointed  out  that  his  client  has  criminal
1

--- Page 923 ---
SLP(Crl.)  No.9965/2026
antecedents.
2. He  further  brought  to  our  notice  that  the  original
complainant has passed away.  However, what is important to
note that the FIR is of the year 2018.  The petitioner came to
be arrested in connection with the alleged offence after a
period of 07 years.  According to him, the petitioner was not
absconding at any point of time. We would like to know from the
State why it took seven years to arrest the petitioner.
3. We permit the learned counsel to implead the legal heirs
of the original complainant as party respondents.
4. Cause title be amended accordingly. 
5. Let notice be issued to the legal heirs of the original
complainant and to the State/respondent also, returnable on
23.07.2026.

8. Liberty is granted to the petitioner to serve notice
through the Standing Counsel for the State/respondent.

                  O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. On 11.02.2026, we had given an opportunity to the petitioner
to deposit the amount for which the attachment was made which would
be not less than Rs. 1,00,00,000/- (Rupees One Crore).
3. Thereafter on 20.03.2026 again, time was granted to make the
payment. Today also time is being sought for the payment.
4. From the conduct of the petitioner it is clear that on some
pretext or the other, despite assuring the Court and indulgence

--- Page 959 ---
given by this Court, the petitioner is not complying with the
orders of this Court.
5. Accordingly, we find that the petitioner does not deserve any
further indulgence. Even on merits, we don't find any case made out
for grant of anticipatory bail to the petitioner.
6. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
7. The interim protection granted by this Court vide order dated
20.03.2026 stands withdrawn.
8. The original documents submitted by the petitioner before the
court may be returned.
9. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.

Heard learned counsel for the parties. 

--- Page 973 ---
2. At the outset, learned counsel for the petitioner submits that
the facts are clear in the present case inasmuch as the petitioner,
being wife has been denied the company of the husband and the child
has been denied the company of her mother and that the petitioner
has  been  harassed  by  the  father-in-law  and
mother-in-law/respondents No. 2 and 3 and the husband along with
the son are living in Singapore and the petitioner has not had the
occasion to interact with the son as a mother.

respondents No. 2 and 3 submits that the parties came together
briefly on a Zoom meeting and that too as part of an earlier
mediation.

among close family members and there is also a child, the Court may
give mediation a chance.

respective clients, learned counsel submit that the matter be
listed  on  15.07.2026  at  3:00  p.m.  when  the  petitioner,  the
respondents No.2 and 3 as also the husband and son of the

                  O R D E R
Heard the petitioner in-person. 
2. As recorded by this Court in the order dated 02.01.2024, the
petitioner stated that he has no objection to the order granting
divorce in favour of the respondent-wife and is willing to pay in
terms of order dated 21.05.2019, which was upheld by the High Court
on 14.10.2021, subject to the respondent-wife not pursuing the case
under Sections 498A, 406, 506 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860.
3. Despite repeated opportunities being given to the petitioner
vide orders of this Court dated 27.10.2025 and 15.05.2026 to make
the above deposit and despite this Court on the last occasion i.e.
19.05.2025 giving final opportunity to the Petitioner to make the
deposit, the same has not been done.
4. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petitions are dismissed
5. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.

date of listing.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned Senior counsel appearing
1

--- Page 1005 ---
for the petitioner and learned Senior counsel appearing
for the respondent(s).
Though the learned Senior counsel appearing for the
petitioner is correct in his submission made with respect
to the application of Clause 25 of the General Standing
Order 170/2020 dated 14.12.2020, the fact remains that the
official respondents did not intend to renew the license
awarded to the petitioner and, in fact, in the subsequent
tender, the petitioner had participated without prejudice
to any of his contentions.
In such view of the matter, no ground is made out
for interference.
However, the petitioner is granted a period of six
weeks to hand over the vacant physical possession of the
licensed  subject  premises,  subject  to  filing  an
unconditional undertaking on oath before this Court within
a period of two weeks from the date of receipt of a copy
of this order for vacating the subject premises in the
aforesaid terms
The Special Leave Petition stands dismissed with the
aforesaid liberty.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(KRITIKA TIWARI)                                (POONAM VAID)

                   Mr. Akash Srinanda, Adv.

                   Mr. Saurabh Ankit, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned Senior counsel appearing
for the petitioner and learned counsel appearing for the
respondent. 
1

--- Page 1009 ---
The issue which is sought to be raised relies on a
factual premise which pertains to whether the petitioner
before us is a proprietor or an authorized signatory of
the firm.
We are dealing with a case where the petitioner has
not even given a reply, though the learned Senior counsel
appearing for the petitioner submitted that the legal
notice has not been served upon him.
Be that as it may, we are not inclined to go into
the factual adjudication of the dispute at this stage,
especially when we are dealing with proceedings which are
primarily summary in nature.
The Special Leave Petition is dismissed.
All issues to be raised are left open.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(KRITIKA TIWARI)                                (POONAM VAID)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
2

ITEM NO.64               COURT NO.11               SECTION IX-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).38035/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  19-09-2025
in SA No.135/2021 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay
at Aurangabad]
DEEPAK RITHE                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
VILAS & ORS.                                       Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This petition was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Manish Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Aditya Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Hemant Singh Pal, Adv.
                   Mr. Rameshwar Prasad Goyal, AoR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Nishant Ramakantrao Katneshwarkar, AoR
                   
                  O R D E R
At the outset, learned counsel for the parties submit that
though the matter was referred to mediation but the same has
failed. 
2. Having regard to the aforesaid and also the fact that the
learned counsel for the petitioner has restricted his submission
for being referred to mediation, the same having failed, in the
opinion of the Court, the matter now requires to be given a
quietus.
3. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.

4. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.66               COURT NO.11               SECTION II-E
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).10682/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  19-06-2023
in CRLP No.100576/2023 passed by the High Court of Karnataka
Circuit Bench at Dharwad]
YUVARAJ NIMBALKAR                                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA                             Respondent(s)
IA No. 165875/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 165877/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE
DEFECTS
IA No. 165874/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Mahesh Thakur, AoR
                   Mr. Narveer Yadav, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddhartha Sati, Adv.
                   Ms. Ruchi Kumari, Adv.
                   Mr. Dushyant Pratap Singh, Adv.
                   Dr. Anthony Raju, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                  O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
2. Delay condoned.
3. Having considered the matter in its entirety and going through
the material on record, we are not inclined to grant bail to the
petitioner, though he may have been under incarceration for more
than five years.
4. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.

--- Page 1049 ---
5. However, in view of what has been contended by learned counsel
for the petitioner that the injured person has been evading his
examination before the Court for the last one and a half years, we
find that the State of Karnataka has been slack in its duty towards
the system by ensuring that witnesses are produced on time and the
trial is concluded at the earliest, more so, when the accused is in
incarceration.
6. Accordingly, we direct the State of Karnataka to ensure that
the said victim is examined before the Court below within a period
of three weeks from today. The Trial Court shall also ensure the
same.
7. Registry is directed to transmit a copy of this order to the
Superintendent of Police, District Koppal, Karnataka.
8. Needless to say that if thereafter, an application for bail is
filed by the petitioner, the same shall be considered on its own
merits in accordance with law.
9. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA) (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)              ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

therefor;
(v) if the trial has commenced, the number of witnesses
examined;

(vii) whether any co-accused is enlarged on bail; and
(viii) any other information relevant for a decision on the
special leave petition.

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 22-01-2026
in CRMM No.32834/2021 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana
at Chandigarh]
SUNDER SINGH                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
SUKHBIR SINGH & ORS.                               Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION) 
(IA No. 165470/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Kartar Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Ajit Kumar Ekka, AoR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
2. Having considered the matter in its entirety and going through
the material on record, we do not find any ground warranting
interference by this Court. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition
stands dismissed.
3. However, we record a finding that the allegations are totally
frivolous and clearly made with an intention of abusing the process

--- Page 1131 ---
2
of the Court.
4. After  the  order  was  dictated,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner drew the attention of this Court, to an earlier order
passed by the High Court of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh in
CRM-M-32834  of  2021  dated  29.11.2021,  where  the  following
observation has been made:-
“It is directed that Superintendent of Police, Hisar will
ensure that in case any such complaint, under Section 3 of
the SC/ST, is filed by respondent no.2/complainant, no FIR
would be registered unless an opinion from the District
Attorney (Legal) is obtained in this regard.”
5. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that the above
observation passed by the High Court is totally illegal and beyond
jurisdiction  as  there  ought  not  be  any  bar  to  any  person
approaching the police and the police should not be estopped from
proceeding in the matter as per the Statute.
6. We find substance in the said contention of the learned
counsel for the petitioner.
7. Accordingly, taking suo motu cognizance of the same, the said
portion of the order dated 29.11.2021 passed by the High Court of
Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh in CRM-M-32834 of 2021 stands set
aside.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 1132 ---
1
ITEM NO.36               COURT NO.11               SECTION II-D
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).1954/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 27-01-2025
in RPFC No.16/2024 passed by the High Court of Kerala at Ernakulam]
JAYAPRAKASH E.P.                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
SHENEY P. & ANR.                                    Respondent(s)
(IA No. 15919/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT)
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Basil Jaison, Adv. 
    Mr. Jose Abraham, AoR
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Ashok Anand, Adv.
                   Mr. Satyakam Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Ajay Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Sandeep Singh, AoR                   
                   
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. At the outset, learned counsel for the petitioner submits that
the Court may refer the matter to mediation.
3. Learned counsel for the respondents agrees to the same.
4. Accordingly, the matter is referred to the Supreme Court
Mediation  Centre  (SCMC).  Initially,  the  parties  as  also  the

--- Page 1133 ---
2
daughter residing with the petitioner are directed to appear before
the  Coordinator  of  the  SCMC  through  video  conferencing  on
14.07.2026 at 02:30 p.m.  Thereafter, the learned Mediator shall
take a call, as to whether, the parties are required to be
physically present or not.
5. However,  if  the  respondents-wife  and  elder  daughter
respectively are required to come to Delhi to attend the mediation
proceedings,  they  shall  be  entitled  to  reimbursement  towards
boarding,  lodging  and  transportation  which,  we  quantify  at
Rs.15,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Thousand) per visit.
6. The same shall be deposited into the bank account of the
respondent no.1 directly by the petitioner at least, four days
prior to the date fixed on which the respondents have to physically
appear before the learned Mediator.
7. Details of the bank account of respondent no.1 shall be
provided by the learned counsel for the respondents to the learned
counsel for the petitioner, during the course of the day.
8. Learned Mediator is requested to submit a report within a
period of two months from the date of first appearance of the
parties before the learned Mediator.
9. List on 30.09.2026.
10. In the meantime, to facilitate the mediation, all  inter se
proceedings pending between the parties shall remain stayed.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).10402/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 13-05-2026
in CRMM No.26833/2026 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana
at Chandigarh]
SUNDRI                                             Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA                                   Respondent(s)
(IA No. 168323/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT
IA No. 168325/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Ms. Kajal Bhatia, Adv.
    Mr. Rishi Kapoor, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankur Gogia, Adv.
                   Mr. Rahul Kumar, Adv.                   
                   Mr. Chand Qureshi, AoR
                   Mr. Santosh Anandrao Jadhav, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Anurag Kulharia, Asst. AG
    Dr. Navya Jannu, Adv.
    Mr. Gyaaneshwar Joshi, Adv.
    Mr. Samar Vijay Singh, AoR
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The petitioner seeks anticipatory bail in connection with FIR
No.0050 of 2026 dated 24.02.2026, registered at Police Station GRP
Ambala  Cantt.,  District  GRP  Ambala  Cantt.  for  the  offence

--- Page 1139 ---
2
punishable under Section 20(b)(ii)(c) of the Narcotic Drugs and
Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the petitioner
was not named in the FIR and was also not caught with any
contraband and was implicated in the case only upon the disclosure
statements of the accused persons (A1 & A2) from whom, recovery was
made.  It  was  further  contended  that  there  is  absolutely  no
connection between the arrested accused persons and the petitioner.
4. Learned counsel for the respondent-State of Haryana submits
that one of the arrested persons has disclosed the name of the
petitioner as the person, who was to be supplied the recovered
contraband by the accused persons.
5. Having considered the matter in its entirety and going through
the material on record, we find that no case for grant of
anticipatory bail to the petitioner is made out. Accordingly, the
Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

                   Mr. Kumar Nikhil, Adv.
                   Mr. Rahul Gupta, AOR                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. By these Applications, the applicant (the original petitioner)
has prayed for the following:-
Miscellaneous Application No.1784/2026:-
(i) Allow the present Application and modify the Order dated
13.05.2026  qua  the  condition  of  pre-deposit  of  5%  and/or

--- Page 1159 ---
2
permitting the Petitioner to file statutory appeals without pre-
deposit against the adjudication orders for FY 2019-20, 2021-22,
2022-23 and 2023-24 passed by the Respondent within the statutory
time period prescribed under Section 107 of the CGST Act from the
and/or remand the matter back for adjudication after affording the
Petitioner a reasonable opportunity of being heard and/or ; 
(ii) Pass such other and further order(s) as this Hon'ble Court
may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the
case, in the interest of justice.
Miscellaneous Application No.1785/2026:-
(i) Allow the present application and modify the order dated
13.05.2026 qua the condition of pre-deposit of 5% and permitting
the Petitioner to file appeals without pre-deposit and /or quash
and set aside the Adjudication Order dated 02.07.2025 passed by
the Respondents and remand the matter back for adjudication after
affording the Petitioner a reasonable opportunity of being heard,
and/or;
(ii) Pass such other and further order(s) as this Hon’ble Court
may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of
the case.
2. We outright reject the prayer insofar as doing away with
condition of pre-deposit of 5%, however, the Statutory Appeals may
be preferred within the statutory time period as provided under
Section 107 of the Central Goods and Services Act, 2017.
3. It’s upto the applicant whether to prefer any Statutory Appeal
or avail any other remedy in law. 
4. With  the  aforesaid,  the  Miscellaneous  Applications  and
Interlocutory Applications are disposed of.
5. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
 (VISHAL ANAND)                                    (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  14-05-2026

YAHYA RAVISH KHOT                                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
NATIONAL INVESTIGATION AGENCY                      Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 168642/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s)  Ms. Sridevi Panikkar, AOR
                   Mr. Abhimanue Shrestha, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Learned counsel appearing for the petitioner submits that
despite the earlier order passed by this Court, the writ petition
filed by the petitioner way back in the year 2024 challenging the
legality of arrest and the subsequent orders of remand is yet to
see the light of the day. 
The petitioner has filed a writ petition touching upon
his liberty. In a matter like this, we expect the High Court to
prioritize  such  cases  as  against  the  numerous  other  pending
matters. 
In such view of the matter, we are inclined to issue a

--- Page 1177 ---
2
positive direction to the High Court to make an endeavour to
dispose of Writ Petition (Crl.) No.3291/2024 one way or other
within a period of three months from the date of receipt of a copy
of this order.  
The Special Leave Petition stands disposed of. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.    
(SWETA BALODI)                                  (POONAM VAID)

STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.                             RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
We have heard the learned counsel appearing for the
parties. 
We are not inclined to entertain this Transfer Petition. 
However, the appearance of petitioner Nos. 2 and 3, who
are the parents of the petitioner No.1, is dispensed with unless
and  until  it  is  specifically  required  by  the  Trial  Court.
Similarly, the appearance of the respondent No.2 is also dispensed
with on the same terms. 
The Transfer Petition is, accordingly, dismissed. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

treating it as a precedent to be followed in other instances, the

are expected to be brought back within ten days.

1. We are not inclined to transfer Petition-A No. 773 of 2024 titled “Meghna
Arun  Bagree  vs. Arun Shreelal Bagree” pending  before  the  Principal  Judge,
Family Court, Nashik, Maharashtra to the Court of the Principal Judge, Family
Court, Surat, Gujarat; hence, the transfer petition is dismissed.
2. Pending interlocutory application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(JATINDER KAUR)                                 (NAND KISHOR)
